lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: Revocation correctness depends on a CDC fast-path the plan never bounds, and the 'read-time re-check makes correctness independent of propagation latency' claim is only HALF true. Read-time re-check at the owning node protects the DRILL-DOWN path (Section 4.3 mechanism 2). But the central discovery index itself is populated by Outbox+CDC and CAN surface a confidential-adjacent record's EXISTENCE/metadata that should have been pulled after a grant or classification change. The plan admits 'stale shareable metadata after revocation is a leak vector' (10.2) then never specifies how a REVOKE or a tier reclassification (private->confidential, or un-sharing previously-published metadata) is propagated to DELETE from the central index. CDC is append-only log-based; tombstone/delete propagation, ordering vs the original publish, and the window during which the central index still serves a now-revoked pointer are all unspecified. A revoke that fast-paths the GRANT tuple (so drill-down denies) but leaves the metadata row in the central index still leaks the fact-of-existence and the publishable abstract/fingerprint — which for an export-controlled or confidential project IS the leak.
why: Confidentiality is the locked, first-class, existential requirement. 'Read-time re-check' only covers the byte fetch, not the metadata that was already replicated outward. There is a real, unmodeled staleness window in the central index for revocations and reclassifications. This is exactly the new-enemy problem the plan congratulates itself on solving — but it solved it for the authz check, not for the replicated index.
fix: Specify the revocation/reclassification path for the central index explicitly: (a) emit CDC tombstones for un-publish/revoke/reclassify, with a monotonic per-record version (e.g., grantor-node Lamport/HLC) so consumers reject out-of-order resurrections; (b) make the central index entry itself carry a short TTL / lease so it self-expires absent re-publish, bounding worst-case staleness even if a tombstone is lost; (c) gate any surfacing of a metadata hit through a lightweight 'still-publishable?' check that can be answered from a fast, strongly-consistent revocation set (not the eventually-consistent index); (d) define and SLO the metadata-revocation propagation bound and treat exceeding it as a P1 incident, since 'read-time re-check' does NOT cover it.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: SpiceDB is drawn as a single, globally-consistent, cross-tenant relationship store in the SHARED control plane, and every confidential drill-down does a ZedToken-consistent Check against it. This makes SpiceDB (a) a global consistency bottleneck and single failure domain for ALL cross-institution access, and (b) a cross-region latency tax on the hot path. The plan's own Section 7.4 says 'confidential PDP evaluation executes inside the owning cell ... never depends on the shared plane' — but Section 4.3 mechanism 2 shows PEPB calling SPICE (control plane) for the grant Check on every drill-down. These two statements contradict: the confidential check DOES depend on the shared plane for the grant relationship. If SpiceDB is down or partitioned, either cross-institution confidential access fails closed (federation outage) or you fall back to cached/eventual (re-introducing new-enemy). For an EU cell drilling into a US-owned record, the ZedToken-consistent Check is a cross-region round-trip on the synchronous path.
why: It collapses the control/data-plane isolation story the whole architecture rests on, makes the 'confidential checks never depend on the shared plane' claim false, and turns a single SpiceDB cluster into the availability and latency ceiling for the entire federation. Strong consistency (ZedToken) at global scale across regions is precisely where SpiceDB/Zanzibar latency blows up — and the plan defers benchmarking it to Open Question #5, i.e., the core security path is unvalidated.
fix: Resolve the contradiction: either (a) replicate/cache the relevant sharing_grant tuples INTO each owning cell with a strongly-consistent, monotonic mechanism (cell-local SpiceDB with grant tuples pushed via authenticated, ordered replication + ZedToken minted at the grant's home), so the confidential Check is cell-local and survives control-plane partition; or (b) explicitly accept that cross-institution confidential access has a hard dependency on the exchange plane and define its multi-region SLO, partition behavior (fail-closed), and the SpiceDB topology (single global cluster vs regional clusters + cross-region grant replication). Benchmark ZedToken p99 across regions at realistic fan-out BEFORE committing the 50ms SLO; that spike belongs in Phase 0, not Open Question #5.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: The central metadata/discovery index is repeatedly described as 'thin', 'stateless', 'modest fixed cost' (Sections 4.1, 16.1) — but it is the fan-in of EVERY institution's publishable profiles, abstracts, capability tags, AND embeddings of publishable text, plus pointers. That is a large, growing, write-amplified multi-tenant vector+text index, not a thin stateless service. Its growth is the UNION of all nodes' public-tier corpora (OpenAlex-scale per institution x N institutions), it ingests a continuous CDC firehose from N nodes, and it must serve cross-institution discovery search with the 800ms p95 SLO. There is no sharding model, no per-tenant partitioning strategy for it, no embedding-dimension/RAM budget, no write-throughput-vs-query-latency tradeoff, and no statement of whether it is Qdrant/OpenSearch and how it scales horizontally. Calling the control plane 'mostly stateless' is simply wrong given this index lives there.
why: Horizontal scalability is a hard non-functional requirement. The single largest stateful component in the system is mislabeled as thin and left unsized. At N=50 R1s each with ~50k–500k publishable works/profiles plus embeddings, this is tens-to-hundreds of GB of vector+text index with a heavy continuous write load — its scaling determines control-plane cost and the discovery SLO, and it's currently a black box.
fix: Model the central index as a first-class stateful, horizontally-sharded store: choose the engine, define shard key (by tenant? by topic? by embedding cluster?), state the embedding dimension after MRL truncation, estimate index size and write QPS at N=10/50/200 nodes, and define how query latency holds under continuous CDC ingest. Decide whether discovery search is scatter-gather across shards (and bound that fan-out latency) or pre-aggregated. Remove 'thin/stateless/modest' framing for the control plane and give it a real capacity model.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: 'Brokered drill-down' (Section 4.3 mechanism 2, 14.2) is a synchronous cross-node RPC whose availability and latency are governed by the OWNING node — yet the plan's failure-domain analysis (14.3) claims clean cell isolation and the SLOs (14.4) treat operations as independent. A drill-down from A into B's record requires A's PEP, the control plane (SpiceDB + index), AND B's cell to all be up: availability is the PRODUCT, not the min. Self-hosted/on-prem sovereign nodes (Section 13) make this worse — they may be air-gapped, behind university firewalls, on flaky campus networks, or simply offline at 2am. The plan acknowledges 'slowest-node tail latency' once (14.2) and then sets a discovery p95 of 800ms that only holds for the prebuilt-index path, not the brokered path, whose tail is unbounded by design (it waits on an arbitrary university's node).
why: Cross-institution drill-down is a core feature (discovery, workspaces). If its latency/availability is dominated by the least-reliable participating node and that's not modeled, the federation feels broken precisely when it spans many heterogeneous, university-operated nodes — which is the GTM endgame (consortium, sovereign editions). 'Cell-isolated failure domains' is true for intra-node ops but false for the cross-node feature that justifies the product.
fix: Separate SLOs for index-path vs brokered-path operations. Give brokered drill-down explicit timeout/circuit-breaker/degraded-result semantics ('owning node unreachable -> show publishable metadata only, mark detail unavailable'). Model cross-node operation availability as a product and state the target (e.g., a brokered request touching 3 components at 99.9% each is ~99.7%). Add per-owning-node health/SLA tracking and define behavior when a sovereign/air-gapped node is offline. Bound the brokered tail explicitly rather than implying the 800ms applies.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: Hot-tenant / hot-partition handling is essentially absent despite the prompt and locked requirements making it obvious (a handful of mega-R1s like MIT/Stanford will dwarf a small college in corpus size, query volume, and publishable-metadata firehose). The plan's only multi-tenancy lever is 'dedicated cell vs shared cell' (Section 2.3) and 'per-tenant rate limits' (14.2). But (a) the SHARED control-plane components — central index, SpiceDB, TEE broker, CDC pipeline — are pooled across ALL tenants and have no noisy-neighbor isolation story; a single mega-tenant's CDC backfill or query storm degrades discovery for everyone. (b) Shared/soft-isolated PLG cells (Lab edition) explicitly co-tenant multiple labs — yet there's no per-tenant resource quota for vector RAM or local-LLM GPU time, which Section 16/Open-Q4 admit is the dominant COGS. A single PLG lab running a large batch distill can starve co-tenants' inference.
why: Horizontal scalability with heterogeneous tenants is locked. Without hot-tenant isolation, the shared planes become the contention point and the multi-tenant PLG economics (the MVP wedge revenue) break under one noisy lab. This is a classic SaaS-at-scale failure the plan doesn't engage.
fix: Add an explicit noisy-neighbor model: per-tenant quotas and fair-scheduling on the central index write/query path and on shared-cell GPU/vector RAM; CDC per-tenant partitioning with bounded lag SLOs so one tenant's backfill can't starve others; a 'cell-split / promote to dedicated' trigger when a tenant crosses a size/QPS threshold. Define what happens to SpiceDB and the TEE broker under a single tenant's load spike. State the shared-cell isolation guarantee precisely (it currently undermines the 'confidential' claim — see Open Q4).

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability
issue: Per-tenant knowledge-graph and vector-index GROWTH inside each cell is unmodeled beyond 'MRL truncation + Qdrant sharding'. Apache AGE (Cypher-on-Postgres) is chosen for the per-cell graph, but AGE's traversal performance on multi-hop queries over a full institutional + ingested-OpenAlex-subset graph (millions of author/paper/citation edges) is known to degrade badly compared to native graph engines — and the plan layers HippoRAG2 Personalized-PageRank ON TOP of AGE in Phase 2. PPR over a large graph in Postgres is not free; '~1k tokens/query' bounds the LLM cost, not the graph-compute cost. There's no statement of graph size per cell, no index strategy for the citation edges, and no acknowledgment that co-locating pgvector + AGE + authz tables in one Postgres (Section 6.2/12) couples three very different workloads (HNSW vector RAM, graph traversal CPU, OLTP authz) onto one instance's resource envelope.
why: Graph + vector growth and cross-node latency are explicitly in scope for this lens. Choosing AGE 'because it co-locates with pgvector and authz' optimizes for deployment tidiness at the cost of the actual scaling characteristic of the graph workload. If multi-hop PPR queries are slow or the co-located Postgres becomes a resource-contention single point per cell, the lit-intelligence/discovery latency SLOs are at risk as corpora grow.
fix: Size the per-cell graph (edges/nodes at a realistic R1) and benchmark AGE multi-hop + PPR latency at that scale before locking it; have a real fallback trigger to Neo4j/Memgraph if AGE doesn't hold (the plan lists them as fallbacks but with no switch criterion). Reconsider co-locating HNSW vector + graph traversal + authz OLTP in one Postgres — define resource isolation or split them. State the graph-compute (not token) cost of HippoRAG2 PPR per query at target scale.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The entire confidentiality guarantee is single-point-of-failure on the classification engine, with no defined behavior for misclassification, classification failure, default tier, or derived-data tiering. The plan says 'classifies every record as public|private|confidential; emits the routing attribute that binds everything' (Section 3.2, 8.1) but never specifies: (a) what tier a record gets if classification fails or is ambiguous (fail-open to public = catastrophic leak; the only safe default is confidential/deny); (b) how a record gets RE-classified when its sensitivity changes (a public preprint that becomes ITAR-controlled, a private doc shared into a confidential project); (c) the tier of DERIVED artifacts — embeddings of confidential text, LLM summaries/research-cards, graph edges, BM25 term-frequency statistics, expertise fingerprints. A confidential document's embedding or its co-author graph edge can leak the underlying content (embedding-inversion attacks are real) yet may be tagged public-by-omission and flow out the Outbox.
why: If classification is the binding attribute for the model router, the Outbox egress filter, and every PEP decision, then a single mis-tag or an untagged derivative silently defeats every downstream control — local-model routing, egress block, and central-index exclusion all key off the same tag. The plan's own invariant ('confidential bytes never leave their cell') is only as true as the weakest classifier decision, and classifiers are probabilistic. There is no second, independent gate. This can sink the platform on first incident.
fix: Make classification fail-CLOSED: any record without a high-confidence tier defaults to confidential (deny egress). Define an explicit derived-data tiering rule: every derivative inherits the MAX tier of its inputs (embedding of confidential text is confidential; a graph edge touching a confidential node is at least private). Add a re-classification/declassification workflow with mandatory human approval for any tier-DOWN transition, audited. Add an independent egress-classification scan (Section 8.4 mentions output scanning — extend it to the Outbox path) so the central index is gated by a SECOND mechanism, not just the ingest tag.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The 'publishable metadata only' invariant on the Outbox->CDC->central-index path is asserted but has no independent enforcing mechanism — the Outbox writer is a TRUSTED component, not a CHECKED one. Section 4.2/5.2 list 'IPublishMetadata (Outbox writer)' as a kernel contract and Section 10.2 says 'Confidential bytes never enter the outbox,' but nothing validates this at the egress boundary. If any module (or a bug, or a misclassified record per Finding 1) writes confidential content to the outbox, Debezium faithfully replicates it to the central control plane, which by design 'holds NO confidential bytes.' The CDC layer cannot tell publishable from confidential — it ships whatever is in the outbox table.
why: This is the existential federation-seam leak (the plan's own Risk #1) but the mitigation is circular: the invariant is enforced by 'we only write publishable things to the outbox,' which is exactly the thing that needs enforcing. A single buggy module, a schema-evolution mistake, or a misclassified field leaks confidential bytes off-tenant into a centralized store, and CDC makes it instantaneous and replicated. Worse, once in the central index it is outside the owning tenant's cell and outside its key boundary.
fix: Insert an independent, deny-by-default egress filter (a PEP) between the outbox and CDC that re-evaluates each outbound record's classification against a strict allowlist schema of publishable fields, strips/rejects anything else, and audits every emission. Treat the outbox as an untrusted source. Add the federation-seam golden-path leak test (Section 15.3 mentions it) as a CONTINUOUS runtime assertion, not just a CI test — sample outbound records in production and alarm on any confidential tag reaching the boundary. Consider per-field classification, not per-record, so a private doc with one public abstract publishes ONLY the abstract.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The central metadata/discovery index is an unencrypted cross-institution aggregation honeypot living OUTSIDE every tenant boundary, with no stated access-control, encryption-at-rest, or tenant-scoping model. Section 4.1/4.2 place 'publishable profiles, abstracts, capability tags, embeddings of publishable text' in the shared control plane. Even if every record is correctly public-tier, the AGGREGATE — who-works-on-what across all institutions, capability tags, embeddings — is a high-value intelligence target (competitive, foreign-adversary, export-control reconnaissance). The plan never says the central index enforces per-query authz, never says it is encrypted, never scopes which tenant can see which other tenant's published metadata (publishing to discovery != consenting to be discovered by EVERY connected institution).
why: Aggregation of individually-public data creates confidential insight (the mosaic effect — well-recognized in export-control and intelligence contexts). A breach of the central index, or an over-broad query, exposes the entire federation's research-capability map at once. For an export-controlled/defense beachhead (Section 3.4) this aggregate is itself sensitive even when each row is unclassified. There is also no notion that University A might publish to the discovery layer but only want Universities in its consortium (not all N nodes) to see it — discovery visibility is treated as binary public, ignoring institution-scoped or consortium-scoped discoverability.
fix: Apply a PEP to the central index itself: queries are authz-checked, results are scoped by a discoverability grant (who may discover this record), and the index is encrypted at rest with control-plane keys distinct from tenant keys. Add a 'discoverability scope' attribute to published records (public-web | federation-wide | named-consortium | named-tenants) enforced at query time. For defense/export-controlled tenants, allow opting OUT of central indexing entirely (live federated drill-down only). Treat the aggregate as at least private-tier and threat-model the mosaic effect explicitly.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: Revocation is correct only for FUTURE reads; the plan has no story for already-disclosed, cached, derived, or in-flight data after a grant is revoked. Section 4.3/7.2/14.4 repeatedly state 'reads re-check at the owning node -> stale index entries can never grant access' and 'read-time re-check makes correctness independent of propagation latency.' True for the next read. But: (a) the central index entry built WHILE a now-revoked grant was active persists until CDC catches up — and that entry is metadata the grantee already has; (b) embeddings or PSI overlaps computed during the grant window are already in the grantee's possession or were used to synthesize an answer; (c) a synthesized LLM answer or downloaded artifact from before revocation is gone from the owner's control entirely. 'Revocable' is sold as a moat and a compliance feature, but it only revokes prospective access, not disclosure.
why: Buyers (and FERPA/GDPR/DUA obligations) will hear 'revocable sharing grants' as 'I can pull back what I shared.' For confidential project IP and PII this gap is both a security and a legal-representation risk. GDPR right-to-erasure (Section 11.3) is reconciled via crypto-shred for data the owner holds, but says nothing about copies legitimately disclosed to a grantee tenant under a since-revoked grant — those live in the grantee's cell under the grantee's keys.
fix: Define revocation semantics precisely and state them in the product: revocation = no further access + central-index entry purged on fast path (not lazy CDC) + obligation on grantee tenant to crypto-shred derived copies (enforceable only via DUA + audit, so say so). Distinguish 'revoke access' from 'revoke disclosure' explicitly. For confidential workspaces, prefer zero-copy query-where-data-resides (the plan mentions this) so no durable copy ever lands in the grantee cell — make that the DEFAULT for confidential cross-tenant work, not an option.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: ABAC+ReBAC composition order and fail-mode are under-specified, creating a fail-open risk. Section 7.2/7.4 describe a 'two-stage check: SpiceDB relationship path -> Cedar ABAC caveat' and say ABAC attributes 'gate whether a relationship even counts.' But the PIP that supplies ABAC attributes (export_controlled, contains_PHI, residency_region, nationality) is a separate per-cell service (Section 7.4). What happens when the PIP is unavailable, returns a missing attribute, or returns stale data? If a missing 'export_controlled' attribute is treated as 'none' (the enum default in Section 6.1 DOCUMENT), an ITAR record with an unset attribute fails OPEN. The plan does not state that absent/indeterminate attributes deny. Also unclear: is the SpiceDB check authoritative and Cedar only narrows (safe), or can a Cedar caveat ever WIDEN access (unsafe)? Cedar's default-deny helps, but the composition contract is not pinned.
why: The hardest compliance attributes (export control, PHI) are exactly the ones most likely to be unset on legacy/ingested records, and 'unset enum -> none -> allow' is a classic fail-open. For a deemed-export scenario this is a felony-grade leak (foreign national gets controlled technical data because the attribute was null).
fix: Specify: (1) indeterminate/missing ABAC attribute => DENY for confidential and export-controlled tiers (fail-closed PIP); (2) ABAC can only NARROW the ReBAC grant, never widen — make this a tested invariant; (3) PIP unavailability on a confidential check => deny, not cache-fallback; (4) change the DOCUMENT.export_controlled default from an implicit 'none' to 'unknown' which the PEP treats as controlled until adjudicated.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The export-control 'deemed export' design collects nationality as an access attribute but never defines how nationality/citizenship is established, attested, verified, or kept current — and the federated IdP makes this worse. Section 6.1/7.2/11.3 add 'nationality'/'country' as a subject attribute for deemed-export gating 'where lawfully collected.' But federated identity (CILogon -> Keycloak, JIT provisioning, eduPersonScopedAffiliation) does NOT carry verified citizenship; home institutions do not release citizenship in SAML assertions, and self-asserted nationality is worthless for ITAR. The plan routes the deemed-export decision through the owning node's PEP using a subject attribute it has no trustworthy source for. A confused-deputy angle compounds it: University A asserts an affiliation for a user, and University B's PEP must make a US-person determination about A's user with no authoritative citizenship source.
why: ITAR deemed-export violations are criminal. Gating access on an unverified, self-asserted, or absent nationality attribute is worse than not claiming the capability — it creates documented evidence the platform PURPORTED to enforce export control and failed. This directly undermines the high-ACV defense-lab beachhead the plan leans on (Section 3.4).
fix: Do not treat nationality as an SSO-carried attribute. Require export-controlled access to be gated by an explicit, institution-attested US-person determination recorded as a signed assertion by the owning tenant's export-control officer (a sharing_grant caveat with an attestation reference), not by a subject claim. Make export-controlled projects fully opt-in AND require the owning node to enumerate authorized persons explicitly (allowlist), never an attribute-derived implicit set. State clearly that the platform enforces the access-control mechanism but the institution owns the US-person determination.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: BYOK/HYOK key model has unspecified DEK lifecycle gaps and an inference-time exposure window that undercuts the crypto guarantee. Section 11.1 says 'Revoke KEK = cryptographic lockout / crypto-shred' and 'per-tenant envelope encryption (DEK/KEK) via Vault.' But: (a) for the platform to run RAG/inference over confidential data, the DEK must be available in plaintext in cell memory — so 'we never can decrypt' (HYOK, Section 13.2) is false during active processing; the plan does not address protecting plaintext-at-use (the vLLM process, the embedding index, OS swap, core dumps). (b) Crypto-shred only erases data encrypted under that KEK — but embeddings in Qdrant, BM25 postings in OpenSearch, and graph nodes in AGE are typically NOT individually envelope-encrypted; the plan never says these stores are encrypted under the tenant KEK, so KEK revocation does NOT shred the searchable derivatives. (c) No key rotation, no DEK-per-record vs DEK-per-tenant decision, no story for what happens to in-flight queries at revocation.
why: Crypto-shred is the GDPR-erasure and revocation backbone (Sections 4.3, 11.1, 11.3). If the embedding/BM25/graph derivatives are not under the same key, revoking the KEK leaves a fully-searchable, content-revealing copy of 'erased' confidential data in the vector and search indices. And 'university holds the key, we never decrypt' (the sovereign-edition sales claim) is contradicted by the need to decrypt for inference.
fix: State the plaintext-at-use boundary honestly: confidential plaintext is exposed in cell memory during inference; mitigate with TEE-backed inference (memory encryption), disabled swap, disabled core dumps, and ephemeral DEK caching. Require that ALL derivative stores (Qdrant, OpenSearch, AGE, object storage) for confidential data are encrypted under the tenant KEK so crypto-shred actually shreds them. Define key rotation and DEK granularity. Correct the HYOK claim to 'university controls key custody and can revoke; plaintext is processed transiently in-cell under TEE' rather than 'we never can decrypt.'

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — enforcement of the public/private/confidential model end-to-end, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: PSI/clean-room aggregate outputs and 'embeddings of publishable text' leak confidential information through inference even when raw bytes never move. Section 4.3 mechanism 3 returns 'overlap / aggregate only (never raw sets)' for 'Do A and B share collaborators?' and the privacy-tech ranking (4.4) lists '2-party PSI + DP for aggregates.' But DP is mentioned ONCE and never wired in: a bare PSI intersection size, or repeated overlap queries, reconstructs membership (a classic differencing/intersection-attack). Likewise 'embeddings of publishable text' in the central index are subject to embedding-inversion (reconstructing source text) — and if any non-public text's embedding slips in (Finding 1), the embedding itself is a leak.
why: The federation's whole premise is 'compute over confidential data without exposing it.' If aggregate/overlap queries lack a privacy budget, an adversary at one institution mines the membership and relationships of another through repeated 'harmless aggregate' queries — a leak that bypasses every PEP because each individual query is authorized. This is the subtle, hard-to-detect failure that erodes the core claim.
fix: Make differential privacy a REQUIRED, budgeted control on all cross-institution aggregate/PSI outputs, not a footnote: per-tenant-pair privacy budget, minimum aggregation thresholds (k-anonymity floor before any overlap is returned), and query-template pre-approval (the plan mentions templates — make them mandatory and rate-limited). Threat-model embedding inversion: do not publish raw embeddings to the central index for anything but lowest-sensitivity public text, or publish only quantized/dimension-reduced vectors, and never publish embeddings of private/confidential derivatives.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: Module dependency graph contradicts the stated 'a module never imports another module' rule. Section 3.1 lists mod-discovery depending on 'Graph' and mod-funding depending on 'Discovery, Graph'; Section 3.5 says GNN link prediction and cross-institution team assembly (mod-funding) sit 'over the heterogeneous graph' built by discovery; Section 9.3 chains expertise->GNN->team-assembly as one evolving surface. Yet Section 5.7 declares 'A module never imports another module' and 5.2 says modules talk only via events or kernel services. These cannot both be true: mod-funding's team assembly is semantically a consumer of mod-discovery's expertise fingerprints and graph features, not of a kernel primitive. Either the shared graph/expertise surface must be promoted INTO the kernel (making the kernel fatter and the 'thin kernel' claim false), or modules will import each other in practice.
why: This is the central modularity claim of the whole plan ('modular to the bone', LOCKED non-functional requirement). If pillar modules secretly depend on each other's domain models, blast radius is NOT minimal: changing discovery's expertise representation breaks funding, and you cannot remove discovery while keeping funding. The architecture rots exactly where the plan claims it is strongest.
fix: Make the dependency direction explicit and acyclic. Promote the shared scholarly graph + expertise-fingerprint representation to a kernel contract (e.g. IExpertiseGraph) with its own versioned schema, OR define an inter-module event/read-model contract in shared/contracts/ that funding consumes (a published 'expertise read-model' projection), never discovery's internals. Add a CI check that fails on any import edge between modules/ packages. Document the true module DAG with directed edges and prove it is acyclic.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The kernel is presented as 8 separate contracts (IClassifier, IModelRouter, IPolicyEnforcement, IAuditSink, IRetrieval, IPublishMetadata, IIdentityResolution, IEventBus) but they are mutually entangled and versioned as one 'kernel' unit with no independent evolution story. IModelRouter routing decisions depend on IClassifier's tier enum; IPublishMetadata depends on IClassifier output to decide what is FRE-safe; IRetrieval's fusion is 'tenant-local' and the router selects local-vs-cloud per tier inside the retrieval flow; IPolicyEnforcement consumes classification attributes. A change to the tier model (e.g. adding a 4th tier, or sub-classifying confidential by export regime) ripples through classifier, router, publish-metadata, authz, AND audit simultaneously. Section 5.6 says 'kernel contracts are semver; modules pin a compatible major' but says nothing about how the EIGHT kernel contracts version relative to EACH OTHER.
why: A kernel where the classification tier enum is a shared type threaded through 5 contracts is a distributed monolith, not a modular kernel. The single most likely real-world change — refining data classification (the plan itself flags export-control sub-tiers, PHI, residency) — forces a coordinated multi-contract release. That is lockstep coupling masquerading as 8 clean interfaces.
fix: Decouple the tier model from a hardcoded enum into a versioned classification *capability descriptor* that router/publish/authz query rather than switch on. Specify intra-kernel contract compatibility rules (which kernel contracts may change independently vs must move together) and publish a kernel-contract dependency matrix. Consider splitting the kernel into 'policy kernel' (classification/authz/audit — must be coherent) vs 'retrieval/serving kernel' (router/retrieval — can evolve faster) with a narrow, stable boundary between them.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The 'provider-agnostic model router stays swappable' claim is undermined by leaking classification and key-custody semantics into the router. Section 8.2 makes the router enforce 'confidential => in-boundary local only, network egress blocked, per-tenant key-scoped so cloud routing is impossible' AND host guardrails (PII filter, prompt-injection, output classification) AND be 'router-aware' so the eval judge LLM must be the local model on the confidential tier. The router is simultaneously: a routing policy engine, a compliance enforcement point, a guardrail pipeline, and an eval-coupling point. That is four responsibilities in one 'swappable' component. Swapping the router now means re-implementing compliance egress enforcement and guardrails.
why: LOCKED requirement: 'provider-agnostic model router ... ALSO supports bring-your-own provider/keys'. A router that has absorbed egress-blocking, key-scoping, guardrails, and eval-judge selection is not provider-agnostic or swappable — it is a god-object on the confidential critical path. Adding a new provider (the locked BYO requirement) forces re-verification of compliance enforcement, which is exactly the high-stakes path you least want to churn.
fix: Separate concerns: (a) IModelRouter = pure provider selection given (tier, tenant policy, capability needs) returning a provider handle; (b) a separate ITransportPolicy/egress-enforcement layer at the network/key boundary that is provider-independent and tested as a compliance control (the plan already has egress-block CI tests — make THAT the enforcement, not the router); (c) guardrails as a pluggable IGuardrail chain composed around any provider. Then 'swap the router' truly means swap selection logic only.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: Contract versioning has no concrete consumer-contract or compatibility-testing mechanism, so 'minimal blast radius' is aspirational. Section 5.6 / 15.3 say kernel contracts are semver, breaking changes get 'a deprecation window where both are served', events carry schema versions, and there are 'contract tests against shared/contracts/'. But there is no mention of consumer-driven contract testing (e.g. Pact), no per-consumer compatibility matrix, no mechanism to know WHICH modules still depend on a deprecated contract version before retiring it, and 'both are served' implies running two implementations of a kernel service simultaneously with no detail on state/auth coherence across versions.
why: Without machinery that proves a contract change is backward-compatible against the actual set of consumers, teams default to fear-driven lockstep deploys ('change everything together to be safe'). That is precisely how interface-rich architectures rot into distributed monoliths. The plan asserts blast radius is minimal but provides no falsifiable way to verify it.
fix: Specify consumer-driven contract testing (provider verifies against recorded consumer contracts) as a CI gate. Maintain a machine-readable contract-version usage registry (which module pins which kernel-contract major/minor) so a deprecation can be safely retired only when zero consumers remain. Define exactly what 'both served' means for stateful kernel services (authz, audit) — dual-write/dual-read semantics, or a translation shim — because serving two versions of the authz spine has security implications, not just compatibility ones.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: IRetrieval is claimed to be a 'thin clean interface over LlamaIndex' but the plan piles tenant-local fusion weights, MRL truncation, two embedding spaces (Qwen3 + SPECTER2), reranking, Adaptive-RAG difficulty routing, HippoRAG2 PPR, CRAG corrective loops, and per-tenant learned convex fusion behind it (Sections 9.1-9.4). These are not implementation details hidden behind one interface — they have divergent inputs/outputs (a corrective loop re-queries; PPR needs the graph; learned fusion needs click feedback state; difficulty routing changes control flow). A single IRetrieval signature cannot stay 'thin' across single-shot hybrid AND multi-hop graph AND corrective-loop AND multi-agent decomposition without becoming a leaky catch-all.
why: LOCKED requirement: retrieval must stay swappable/pluggable. If IRetrieval is thin, it cannot express PPR/CRAG/learned-fusion; if it is rich enough to express them, it is no longer thin and swapping the engine means re-implementing all of it. Either way the abstraction leaks, and the 'defer the rest as pluggable modules behind IRetrieval' promise (9.4) is unsubstantiated — the plan never shows the interface tolerates these as drop-ins.
fix: Define IRetrieval as a small stable core (query -> ranked passages with scores+provenance) and model the advanced behaviors as a composable RETRIEVAL PIPELINE of stages (retriever -> fuser -> reranker -> corrective-loop -> graph-augment), each its own micro-interface, so CRAG/PPR/learned-fusion are pipeline stages not IRetrieval reimplementations. State-bearing concerns (per-tenant learned fusion weights, click feedback) must be an explicit IFeedbackStore dependency, not hidden inside a 'thin' retriever. Show the worked interface for at least the single-shot and the corrective-loop case to prove the seam holds.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, coupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The federation seam — explicitly the existential risk (#1) — has no testable contract for what 'publishable metadata only' means, so the most important boundary in the system is unenforceable by construction. Section 4.3/10.2/15.3 rely on 'only FRE-safe / explicitly-shared records flow out via Outbox+CDC' and 'golden-path leak tests' that 'confidential bytes never appear in the outbox'. But the determination of what is publishable is made by IClassifier + per-record tier, and the Outbox is written by the Dagster pipeline (Section 10.1, CLASSIFY -> OUTBOX). There is no schema-level guarantee, no allowlist of publishable field projections, no type-system separation between a 'confidential record' and its 'publishable projection'. A new module or a schema change that adds a field to the outbox event can silently leak.
why: This is a LOCKED requirement ('confidentiality is a first-class enforced concept end to end') and the plan's own #1 existential risk. 'Tests assert confidential bytes never appear' is a negative test against an open-ended space — it can only catch known leak shapes, never prove absence. Maintainability angle: every future field added to any entity is a potential leak, so the blast radius of ANY data-model change extends to the federation seam. That is the opposite of minimal blast radius.
fix: Make publishability a structural type, not a runtime tag: define an explicit PublishableProjection schema per entity in shared/contracts/ that is an allowlist of fields; the Outbox writer can ONLY emit PublishableProjection objects, constructed via a total function from the internal record (fields default to non-published). Then 'no confidential leak' becomes a type guarantee plus a single audited projection function, not an infinite negative test surface. Add a CI gate that any new field on an internal entity is non-publishable unless explicitly added to the projection with a classification review.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The MVP wedge delivers none of the moat and lands the company in the single most commoditized, outfunded arena. The plan's own positioning (Section 1.4, 2.5) repeatedly says 'don't lead with public-literature AI — commoditized, outfunded.' Yet the actual MVP (Section 3.3) is exactly that: single-tenant grounded Q&A + semantic search + expert discovery over one lab's own corpus, public+private tiers, confidential merely 'defined in schema + demoed.' At first revenue the product IS a single-tenant RAG-over-papers tool competing head-on with Elicit, Consensus, SciSpace, Scholar-QA, Undermind — all better-funded, public-data specialists. The federation/confidentiality moat (the entire reason this company exists) is deferred to Phase 2, gated on N>=2 nodes. So the company earns its first dollars in the arena it explicitly says it must avoid, with no differentiation, and asks customers to pay for a worse version of free/cheap incumbents while the actual value proposition is a promise.
why: A wedge that earns revenue but in a commoditized, undifferentiated space produces high churn, no pricing power, and no signal that the real product works. It validates the wrong thing. Worse, it burns the runway and team months building lit-intelligence parity instead of proving the confidential-federation thesis that justifies the raise. If a fundable narrative is 'we own confidential cross-institution federation,' the MVP must demonstrate that, not RAG-over-PDFs.
fix: Re-cut the MVP so first revenue is tied to differentiated value, not commoditized parity. Two credible options: (a) Lead with the confidential single-lab local-model-isolation as a FUNCTIONAL product, not a demo — sell 'your confidential research corpus, AI Q&A, provably never leaves your boundary' to one security-sensitive lab where no public-tool competitor can play; price the security guarantee, not the RAG. (b) Skip true PLG and land a single 2-node consortium design partner (two collaborating labs at different institutions who already co-author) so the federation seam is exercised and de-risked at first revenue. Either way, do not let first revenue come from undifferentiated public-literature Q&A.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Beachhead unit economics are likely inverted and the plan knows it but files it as Open Question #4 instead of resolving it pre-commit. The beachhead is a department/lab at $179-499/mo (Section 3.4), but the confidential value prop requires a local GPU running vLLM (Section 16.1 explicitly names confidential local GPU as 'the COGS driver'). A dedicated isolated cell with a GPU sufficient for an 8B+ embedding model plus a served LLM cannot be delivered for ~$300/mo gross — a single always-on cloud GPU instance alone exceeds that several times over. The plan's escape hatch is 'shared-cell soft-isolation for PLG,' but it then concedes (Open Q#4) this 'may not satisfy confidential claims' — which collapses the differentiation back into the commodity-RAG problem above.
why: If the beachhead is sold at a price that cannot cover its own COGS without abandoning the confidentiality guarantee, then either the company loses money on every beachhead customer or it ships a product indistinguishable from commodity public RAG. There is no price point in the $179-499 band that simultaneously (a) clears below the procurement-committee threshold, (b) funds a dedicated confidential GPU cell, and (c) preserves the 'never leaves your boundary' claim. This is the revenue thesis, and it is structurally unproven.
fix: Resolve this BEFORE building, not after. Concretely: (1) Build a real per-cell COGS model for the smallest viable confidential configuration (smallest acceptable local LLM + embedding + vector RAM + Postgres/AGE/OpenSearch) on actual cloud GPU pricing; (2) If COGS > ~$300/mo, the PLG-confidential beachhead is dead — pivot the beachhead to a higher-ACV institutional or grant-funded buyer (an export-controlled lab or a center with a security-line-item budget) where $2-5k+/mo is defensible and the GPU amortizes; (3) Investigate whether the confidential local model can run on the customer's own hardware (the M4 Max / on-prem path) at PLG tier, shifting COGS to the buyer, and whether that is operationally tolerable for a self-serve motion.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The PLG 'below-committee-threshold' motion directly contradicts the day-one data-governance and security burden the plan itself imposes. Section 2.5 and Risk #4 stake PLG land on staying below the procurement committee (credit card / dept PO). But the moment a PLG lab ingests its OWN internal/private corpus (the actual pitch), the institution's data-governance, IT security, and IRB/research-compliance functions are triggered — FERPA exposure if student data is in the corpus, DPA requirements under GDPR (the plan itself names the university as controller / vendor as processor in 11.3), and IT review for any tool touching institutional data. 'A researcher pays $300 on a department card and uploads the lab's confidential corpus to a third-party SaaS' is precisely the shadow-IT pattern university CISOs exist to stop. The plan's own veto-holder persona (Research IT / CISO, Section 2.1) will block exactly this.
why: The PLG motion is the foundation of the GTM sequence (land -> expand). If self-serve PLG with real institutional data is structurally incompatible with how universities govern data, the 'land below committee' step cannot happen, and the whole sequence collapses to a top-down institutional sale from day one — which has a 6-18 month cycle the lean team and runway are not scoped for.
fix: Be explicit about what data a PLG buyer can use without triggering institutional review. Realistically PLG can only safely operate on (a) public literature and (b) the individual researcher's OWN materials (their drafts, their notes) — NOT institutional/private records. If so, the PLG tier is again commodity public RAG (see critical #1). Either (1) accept PLG is public-tier-only and find differentiation elsewhere for that tier, or (2) accept that the confidential pitch is inherently a top-down institutional sale and scope runway/team/cycle-length accordingly. Do not market a self-serve confidential-corpus PLG motion that CISOs will reflexively block.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Network-effect cold-start is asserted, not solved — and the proposed bridge (single-tenant standalone value) is the company's weakest, least-differentiated surface. The moat (Section 1.5) is explicitly 'worthless single-tenant, defensible at N>=2.' The mitigation for the chicken-and-egg (Risk #9, Open Q#3) is 'single-tenant pillars deliver standalone value before exchange flips on.' But those single-tenant pillars are lit-intelligence and intra-institution discovery — precisely the commoditized space (critical #1) where the company is weakest. There is no concrete plan for HOW the first 2 nodes get connected: which two institutions, why they'd connect, what the first cross-institution share is, or how the sharing-grant friction (legal DUA, both CISOs, both research offices agreeing) gets overcome for the very first edge. Cross-institution data sharing between universities is notoriously slow (DUAs take months); the plan needs the SECOND node live and a legal sharing agreement executed before ANY network value appears.
why: Marketplaces/networks die in the cold-start. Here the cold-start is worse than a typical two-sided market because each new edge requires bilateral legal agreements between risk-averse institutions, not just a signup. If the bridge to N>=2 is 'sell undifferentiated single-tenant value first,' and that value can't sustain revenue (Open Q#3 admits this is unvalidated), the company may never reach the N where its actual product exists.
fix: Design the cold-start explicitly. (1) Identify a seed dyad/cluster that ALREADY collaborates and already has a DUA or master agreement (e.g., an existing NSF/NIH multi-site center, a university-system or alliance like a CIC/Big Ten Academic Alliance, a CTSA hub) — sell into that pre-existing collaboration so the legal seam is pre-solved and N>=2 is the FIRST deployment, not a later phase. (2) Make the first cross-institution use case one that needs zero confidential sharing to demonstrate value (public-tier federated expert discovery across the two members), then upsell confidential workspaces. (3) Treat consortia (a locked buyer) as the cold-start solution, not a Phase-3 monetization afterthought.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Pricing benchmarks are borrowed from non-analogous products and the multi-axis model (base + per-active-user-per-module + exchange-add-on + BYO premium) creates quote complexity that fights the PLG self-serve motion. The plan anchors institutional pricing to Starmind (~$4k/mo base) and Interfolio (~$28k/yr) and department pricing to Instrumentl/Atom (~$179-499/mo) — but Starmind is an enterprise expertise-network, Interfolio is faculty-activity-reporting, Atom is a public-data grant tool; none of them carry a per-tenant confidential GPU cell as COGS, so their price points say nothing about what THIS product must charge to be viable (see critical #2). Separately, a four-axis pricing model is a sales-engineering and quoting burden incompatible with credit-card PLG; PLG requires a single, legible, self-serve price.
why: Pricing anchored to the wrong comparables understates required price and overstates achievable margin. A complex multi-axis quote model cannot be sold self-serve, undermining the PLG motion that is the foundation of the GTM sequence. Mispriced + hard-to-quote = slow sales and negative-margin customers.
fix: (1) Derive price bottom-up from the real confidential-cell COGS (critical #2), then validate against willingness-to-pay in beachhead interviews — do not anchor on incumbents in different categories. (2) For PLG, define ONE flat self-serve price with a fixed module bundle; reserve the multi-axis configurable pricing for the institution/consortium sales-assisted motions only. (3) Validate (per Open Q#12) that the per-active-user metric is even acceptable to research buyers, who frequently resist per-seat models for fluctuating lab populations and prefer FTE-capped site licenses.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: The Phase-0 'MVP wedge' is not an MVP — it mandates the entire kernel (classification engine, model router, tenant isolation primitive, SpiceDB+Cedar two-stage authz, append-only tamper-evident audit spine, identity resolution service) PLUS hybrid retrieval + reranking + RAGAS-in-CI + AGE knowledge graph + OpenAlex/ORCID ingestion + SOC2 readiness, all before the first paying lab. That is 6+ distinct infra systems (Postgres/AGE, Qdrant, OpenSearch, SpiceDB, Vault, Keycloak/CILogon) plus a compliance program, justified by the claim these primitives 'cannot be retrofitted' (Risk #7). For a single-tenant PLG lab buying lit-intelligence, most of this is dead weight at ship time.
why: A 3-engineer team building this reaches first revenue in roughly 12-18 months, not the implied fast wedge. The 'can't be retrofitted' argument is doing enormous load-bearing work and is largely false for a single-tenant deployment: SpiceDB ReBAC, cross-tenant grants, the audit spine's tamper-evidence, and the isolation cell boundary deliver ZERO value to one lab querying its own corpus — a single-tenant app needs simple RBAC and a Postgres audit table. You can ship the INTERFACE (IPolicyEnforcement, IAuditSink) as a thin shim over trivial implementations and swap in SpiceDB/tamper-evident logging when N≥2 actually arrives, with bounded blast radius — which is exactly what the plan's own modularity model (Section 5) is supposed to enable. The plan contradicts itself: it preaches pluggable interfaces but then hard-wires production implementations of every kernel service into the MVP.
fix: Cut Phase-0 to: thin IPolicyEnforcement over library-level RBAC (not SpiceDB), IAuditSink over an append-only Postgres table (not a tamper-evident merkle log), classification as a column + a model-router if/else (confidential->local, else cloud), hybrid retrieval+rerank, single-tenant discovery. Defer SpiceDB, Cedar, the cross-tenant grant object, the isolation cell primitive, identity-resolution-as-a-service, and SOC2 to Phase 1 when an institutional buyer actually demands them. Keep the interfaces stable; swap implementations behind them. This is the difference between shipping in 4 months and 16.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: Unit economics are probably inverted and the plan knows it but buries it as Open Question #4. The confidential tier REQUIRES an in-boundary GPU running vLLM (network-egress-blocked, per-tenant key-scoped). A dedicated GPU cell (even one A10G/L4 at ~$400-900/mo cloud, or an A100 far more) cannot be served at the stated PLG seat ($12-20/seat) or department band ($179-499/mo) and remain gross-margin-positive — a single department subscription would be consumed by one idle GPU before any other COGS (Qdrant RAM, Postgres/AGE, OpenSearch, SpiceDB, control-plane share, SOC2 amortization).
why: The ENTIRE GTM wedge (Section 2.5: land PLG/department first, prove the local-model isolation demo) is built on a price point that the COGS structure likely cannot support. If the confidential local-model story is the differentiator AND it requires dedicated GPU AND the beachhead can't afford dedicated GPU, then the wedge either (a) runs at negative margin to acquire logos, burning runway, or (b) uses shared/soft-isolated GPU — which directly undermines the 'confidential data never leaves your isolated boundary' claim that is the whole moat and the strongest HECVAT answer. The plan flags this as 'the riskiest unit-economics question' but proceeds to architect everything around the wedge anyway.
fix: Validate COGS BEFORE committing to the wedge sequence, not after. Concretely: (1) Spec the cheapest viable confidential-inference path — a small quantized model on shared CPU (no GPU) for the demo tier, or batched multi-tenant GPU with cryptographic per-tenant isolation, and prove that still satisfies 'data never leaves boundary'; (2) Reframe the MVP so the confidential tier is BYO-compute / self-host (university pays for its own GPU) at PLG, and we only charge for software — this fixes the margin problem and matches the sovereign-edition philosophy; (3) If neither works, the honest conclusion is the beachhead is NOT a price-sensitive PLG lab but a funded R1 department/institution that can absorb dedicated-cell cost, which changes the entire GTM-sequencing claim.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: The self-hosted / sovereign node operational burden is grossly understated and dumped on university Research IT. Section 13.2 hand-waves the on-prem node as 'University Research IT operates it,' but the node stack is: Kubernetes + Helm sub-charts per module + Vault (HYOK key custody) + vLLM on GPU + SpiceDB + Postgres/AGE + OpenSearch + Qdrant + Debezium/Kafka + egress-block network policy + signed-bundle (cosign) air-gapped update pulls + OpenTelemetry/Prometheus/Grafana/Loki/Tempo. That is a platform-engineering team's full-time job.
why: Most university Research IT orgs cannot operate a multi-service Kubernetes + Kafka + Vault + GPU-inference stack with air-gapped signed updates. This either (a) makes the sovereign edition unsellable without a professional-services arm the founding team doesn't have, or (b) forces the startup to operate it remotely, which contradicts 'no confidential bytes traverse to us' and reintroduces the operational cost the on-prem model was supposed to offload. The day-2 ops (upgrades, SpiceDB schema migrations, Debezium connector failures, GPU driver issues, cert rotation) on a customer-operated air-gapped node will generate a support load that crushes a 3-person team.
fix: Drastically shrink the self-host footprint: collapse to the fewest possible services (e.g., Postgres-with-pgvector-and-AGE as a single datastore eliminating Qdrant+OpenSearch+separate-graph for small nodes; embedded authz instead of SpiceDB; skip Kafka via Postgres-LISTEN/NOTIFY or outbox-polling for low-volume nodes). Ship the node as a single signed appliance image (Docker Compose or a k3s single-binary, not full k8s + Helm + Argo). Defer the sovereign/on-prem edition entirely out of the first 18 months — it is the highest-ops, lowest-count buyer. Sell managed cells first where YOU control the stack.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: SOC2 Type II 'started day one' is both a cost and a timeline trap the plan treats as free. SOC2 Type II requires a 6-12 month observation window (not the '3-6 mo readiness' stated), costs ~$30-60k/yr in audit + tooling (Vanta/Drata ~$10-25k/yr) + significant founder time on policies/evidence, and crucially requires the controls to actually be RUNNING during the observation window — which means the full kernel must be in production early, reinforcing the over-scoped-MVP problem.
why: The plan correctly notes SOC2 'gates the first institutional contract,' but it is positioned as a Phase-0/Phase-1 parallel track for a pre-revenue team. SOC2 Type II evidence collection over a year, on a stack still being built, with a 3-engineer team, is a major distraction and cash drain that competes directly with shipping. The 3-6 month figure conflates Type I (point-in-time) with Type II (observation window). Getting this wrong means promising institutional buyers a report that arrives 6-9 months later than implied.
fix: Sequence it honestly: PLG/department wedge does NOT need SOC2 (it's below committee threshold by the plan's own design — that's the whole point of the wedge). Pursue SOC2 Type I first (point-in-time, weeks not a year) to unblock early institutional conversations, then Type II only once revenue justifies the audit spend and the controls are stable. Budget the real number (~$40-70k/yr all-in) as a hard COGS/opex line in Section 16, which currently lists it only qualitatively.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: Two orchestrators (Dagster AND Temporal) plus Kafka/Redpanda plus Debezium CDC plus a schema registry is over-engineered eventing for an MVP and a permanent ops tax. Section 10.1 justifies both orchestrators as 'complementary,' but for a wedge serving a few labs, Temporal (durable cross-institution workflows spanning days-months) has NO use case until Phase 2 federation exists, and the full Outbox+Debezium+Kafka+schema-registry pipeline only matters once there's a central index to feed (also Phase 2).
why: Running Dagster + Temporal + Kafka + Debezium + a schema registry is at least 5 stateful distributed systems to operate, monitor, upgrade, and debug — each with its own failure modes — for a team of three, in service of an MVP that has one tenant and no federation. This is precisely the 'over-engineering' the plan warns about in Risk #6 but only applies to the AI core, not the infra. Debezium/Kafka in particular is operationally heavy (connector management, offset handling, rebalancing) and is dead weight pre-federation.
fix: MVP uses Dagster ONLY (it's the inherited idiom and covers crawl->distill->embed->index->graph). Replace the entire Outbox+Debezium+Kafka+schema-registry stack with a simple transactional-outbox-polling-to-the-central-index, introduced only in Phase 2 when the central index first exists. Introduce Temporal only when the first cross-institution grant lifecycle is built (Phase 2/3). Each of these is genuinely pluggable per the plan's own modularity claims — so defer them and prove the architecture's modularity is real rather than aspirational.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: TEE / confidential-compute as a Phase-2 commitment carries hidden cost, fragmentation, and ops burden the plan under-prices. Section 19 Open Question #8 admits Nitro (AWS-only) vs SEV-SNP/TDX (multi-cloud/on-prem) fragments the story, but the plan still bakes a TEE match-broker into the core exchange architecture (Section 4.1/4.2). TEE-enabled instances cost a premium, attestation infrastructure is non-trivial to build and operate, and a one-TEE-abstraction-over-three-substrates layer is a multi-month engineering project on its own.
why: The TEE broker is presented as central to the cross-institution privacy story (operator-can't-read-match-inputs), but building and operating attestation across Nitro + SEV-SNP + TDX, plus the PSI implementation, is specialist work that the named 3-engineer founding team (one of whom also owns SOC2, HECVAT, k8s, IaC, BYOK/HYOK, AND TEE per Section 16.2) cannot realistically deliver alongside everything else. This is a staffing/cost mismatch: the security/infra engineer is assigned ~5 jobs.
fix: For early federation (Phase 2), replace the TEE match-broker with a far simpler model: brokered drill-down to the owning node (which the plan already has) + plain 2-party PSI for overlap queries, with the central exchange operating under contractual + audit controls rather than hardware attestation. Add TEE only when a specific buyer's threat model demands operator-zero-trust AND pays for it. Re-staff Section 16: the security/infra role as scoped is 2-3 people, not one; either grow the team in the cost model or cut scope to match three engineers.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: The 'strongly-consistent revocation set' is a global mutable no-list that the design simultaneously requires to be (a) strongly consistent, (b) replicated to every geo-distributed cell AND the central index, and (c) checked inline before surfacing every discovery hit — with a <5s propagation SLO. These properties are in direct CAP tension and the plan never reconciles them. A strongly-consistent set replicated synchronously to EU+US cells means the revoke write blocks on cross-region consensus (kills the O(1) cell-local revoke claim and the revoke-write latency), OR it is asynchronously replicated (then it is NOT strongly consistent at the cell, and a cell can serve a revoked record during the replication gap — exactly the leak the design claims to close). 'Strongly consistent' and 'replicated to cells, checked locally, <5s' cannot both hold under partition.
why: This is the single mechanism the plan elevates to 'correctness, independent of purge lag' and stamps as the P1 security-critical control closing the v1 metadata-leak gap. If its consistency model is internally contradictory, the entire revocation-correctness story collapses back to 'eventual with a hopeful bound' — i.e., the v1 flaw the plan claims to have retracted, now hidden behind a stronger-sounding label.
fix: Pick an explicit model and state its failure behavior. Realistic options: (1) The revocation set is the AUTHORITATIVE strongly-consistent store ONLY at the central index (single region, linearizable), and the index gate is a synchronous read against it — then cell-local replicas are a cache and the cell must fail-closed (deny drill-down) when its replica is staler than the bound, with the bound being a hard liveness check, not a wish. (2) Use a monotonically-growing revocation log with per-record version vectors so a cell can prove freshness; surfacing requires the index to have observed a version >= the record's last-known-revoke-version, else fail-closed. Either way: define what happens to in-flight discovery queries during a revoke-set partition (must be deny/degrade, not stale-serve), and cost the inline lookup (next finding).

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: Per-record HLC monotonicity is the linchpin of revoke-ordering safety (tombstone version > resurrecting publish ⇒ reject), grant-replication monotonicity, and idempotent CDC consumers — yet the nodes generating these HLCs are independently-operated, self-hosted, possibly air-gapped sovereign cells that 'upgrade independently' and may have unsynchronized or adversarially-skewed clocks. HLC bounds skew only if clocks are loosely synced (NTP); a sovereign node with a 10-minute-fast clock can stamp a publish with an HLC that dominates a legitimate later tombstone from the central authority, resurrecting revoked metadata and defeating the ordering guarantee. The plan asserts HLC monotonicity as if it were a closed property of a single logical clock domain; it is not, across mutually-distrusting institutions.
why: Revocation ordering safety is mechanism (b) of the three-mechanism leak defense. If a clock-skewed or malicious node can mint a dominating version, the tombstone-rejection logic becomes an attack vector for un-revoking confidential metadata — turning a safety mechanism into a leak amplifier. This is precisely the kind of cross-trust-boundary timing assumption that breaks federations.
fix: Do not let the data-owning cell be the sole source of the version that orders its own revocation against the authority's tombstone. Anchor revoke/tombstone ordering to a version assigned by the central revocation-set authority (a single logical clock / monotonic counter the authority controls), so a tombstone always carries an authority-monotonic version a node cannot outrun. Bound and clamp accepted HLC skew (reject events from nodes beyond a max-skew window, alarm on it). State the trust model explicitly: nodes are mutually distrusting, so no node's self-asserted timestamp may order a security decision in its own favor.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: The discovery query path's latency is modeled as scatter-gather across tenant shards with bounded fan-out and an 800ms p95 SLO, but the plan adds an inline per-hit revocation-set gate AND a per-query Discovery PEP authz + discoverability-scope filter on top, and never composes these costs. At N=50-200 tenants with topic-routed shards, a single broad discovery query fans out to many shards, each returning candidate hits, EACH of which must then be (a) authz-checked by the Discovery PEP and (b) gated through the revocation set. If the revocation-set check is a remote lookup, that is potentially thousands of serialized/batched lookups per query inside an 800ms budget; if it's a local bloom/set, its memory and freshness at every query node is unaddressed. The 'partial results flagged incomplete on slow/absent shards' escape hatch also silently interacts with security: an incomplete result set could omit a hit that WOULD have been revoked-checked, but more dangerously, the design never says whether a shard that is up but whose revocation-replica is stale is treated as 'incomplete' (safe) or 'complete' (leak).
why: Discovery is feature pillar (1) and the wedge demo. An 800ms SLO that doesn't account for per-hit security gating at fan-out scale is fiction; under load it will either blow the SLO or, worse, the team will move the revocation gate off the hot path to hit the SLO — re-opening the leak. The interaction between 'partial results' and 'stale revocation replica' is an unspecified leak surface.
fix: Cost the gated query end to end: model fan-out width at N=50/200, hits-per-shard, and per-hit PEP+revocation cost; decide where the revocation gate lives (push it INTO the shard's index as a filtered query against a locally-materialized revocation bitmap refreshed within the leak-window, so it's one indexed filter not N lookups). Define the freshness contract for a shard: a shard whose revocation bitmap is staler than the leak-window must return 'incomplete' (fail-closed), and 'incomplete' must be a security state, not just a quality flag.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: Ordered, authenticated grant replication INTO each cell with a home-minted ZedToken and a 'fail-closed if replica stale beyond a bound' is asserted but its liveness mechanics are undefined. How does a cell KNOW its grant replica is stale beyond the bound? SpiceDB ZedTokens give you monotonic read-after-write within a cluster, not a heartbeat proving 'I have seen all grants up to wall-clock now.' To fail-closed on staleness, the cell needs a freshness oracle: either the SoR pushes a monotonic watermark the cell compares against (requires the SoR to be reachable — re-coupling confidential checks to the control plane the design fought to decouple), or the cell uses a local TTL (then a grant created/revoked during a partition is invisible and the 'survives control-plane partition' claim means it survives by serving STALE authz). For revocation specifically, a partition means the cell cannot learn of a new revoke — so 'cell-local check survives partition' is true for the grant-EXISTS case but is a LEAK for the grant-REVOKED case unless the cell fails closed on partition, which contradicts 'confidential intra/cross access continues during control-plane degradation.'
why: This is the core tension of the whole control/data-plane split: you cannot have both 'confidential cross-institution access keeps working during a control-plane partition' AND 'revocations take effect within the leak-window during that same partition.' The plan claims both. One of them must give, and the safe choice (fail-closed on stale grant replica) directly degrades the availability the plan markets.
fix: Make the partition tradeoff explicit and asymmetric: grant-GRANTS may be served from a cell-local replica during partition (availability), but the cell must treat its revocation knowledge as authoritative-only-if-fresh — i.e., require a recent revocation-set watermark to authorize a CONFIDENTIAL drill-down, and fail-closed (deny, return publishable-metadata-only) when the watermark is older than the leak-window. State plainly: during a control-plane/revocation-set partition, confidential cross-tenant drill-down DEGRADES TO DENY, not stale-allow. Update Section 14.3's 'confidential cross-institution access continues' to 'continues for already-granted reads only within the freshness bound; denies beyond it.'

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: The central discovery index capacity model is order-of-magnitude only and omits the write-amplification reality of its own architecture. Every publishable record carries a lease/TTL and 'self-expires absent re-publish.' At 2.5M-25M projections each needing periodic re-publish to stay alive, the steady-state write load is not just 'snapshot-refresh bursts' — it is a continuous re-lease firehose proportional to corpus size / lease duration. A 24h lease on 25M records = ~290 lease-refresh writes/sec floor, BEFORE actual content changes, CDC tombstones, and re-embeddings. The plan waves at 'smoothed by per-tenant CDC partitioning' but never sizes the re-lease load, and OpenSearch is notoriously write-amplifying (segment merges) under high update churn against a vector+BM25 hybrid index. The 30-150GB storage estimate is plausible; the WRITE throughput and merge pressure are the unmodeled bottleneck.
why: The lease mechanism is mechanism (c) of the leak defense AND a continuous cost driver. If lease duration is short (to bound leak), re-publish write load explodes; if long (to reduce writes), the lease backstop is weak and leak-window depends entirely on tombstones+revocation-set. This is a direct tension between the leak-window SLO and index write scalability that determines central-plane COGS and the 800ms discovery SLO (query latency degrades under heavy merge load).
fix: Decouple liveness from re-write: instead of re-publishing records to refresh leases, maintain leases as a separate lightweight expiry index keyed by record-version (a single TTL store / sorted set), so refresh is a cheap timestamp bump, not a full document re-index against the hybrid engine. Size the actual write QPS at N=50/200 for tombstones + content updates + lease refresh, and state the OpenSearch merge/refresh-interval tuning. Reconcile lease duration explicitly against the leak-window SLO (lease is a backstop measured in hours; revocation-set is the fast path measured in seconds — say so).

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation seam, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics, bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency
issue: Cross-region confidential drill-down latency is 'budgeted explicitly' against the 4s Q&A SLO, but the budget is hand-waved as '~1-2 cross-region hops.' A US researcher drilling into an EU-resident confidential detail triggers: token mint (control plane, some region) + brokered request US→EU (cross-Atlantic ~80-150ms RTT) + EU cell-local SpiceDB Check + EU cell-local revocation-set freshness check + EU local vLLM inference (seconds, not ms, for a generated answer) + response cross-Atlantic. The plan correctly removed the cross-Atlantic ZedToken round-trip, but the GENERATED-answer case puts a multi-second TEE-backed local inference behind a cross-region broker, and 'sum against the 4s SLO' is asserted without showing the arithmetic survives when inference alone can be 2-4s on a quantized 7-8B model. Worse, the brokered-path SLO (p95 2.5s, 5s timeout) and the Q&A SLO (4s) describe overlapping flows and are never reconciled for the cross-region GENERATED case.
why: The headline UX (grounded cross-institution Q&A) is exactly the flow that stacks cross-region network + cold-ish local inference + multi-stage authz. If the real p95 is 6-8s, the wedge demo feels broken, and there is no stated fallback (stream tokens? pre-warm? region-affinity routing?). SLOs that don't compose across the actual call graph are not SLOs.
fix: Produce the explicit latency budget for the worst realistic flow (US subject, EU confidential data, generated answer) as a summed table: token mint + cross-region RTT×hops + cell-local Check + revocation freshness + vLLM TTFT + generation + return. Reconcile the brokered-path SLO and the Q&A SLO into ONE number for this composite flow. If it exceeds 4s, state the mitigation (token streaming so perceived latency is TTFT not full-generation; region-affinity so the requesting user is routed to compute near the data; explicit 'cross-region generated answers have a 6s SLO' honesty).

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The 'strongly-consistent revocation set' is the load-bearing correctness gate for the entire metadata-leak story, but it is simultaneously described as 'replicated to the index and to cells' — i.e., it is an eventually-replicated artifact. A replicated copy cannot be strongly consistent at the consumer. The plan never specifies what happens when the revocation-set REPLICA at the central index (or at a cell) is itself stale or unreachable at surface time. If the index checks its local revocation-set replica and that replica has not yet received a just-added revocation, the stale metadata IS surfaced — the exact leak the section claims to close. The '<5s leak-window SLO, P1 if exceeded' applies to purge lag, not to revocation-set replication lag, which is the real exposure.
why: This is the platform's existential invariant ('confidential bytes / publishable projections gated on revoke'). The plan retracted v1's 'correctness independent of latency' claim as half-true, then reintroduced the same flaw one layer down: the gate is only as strongly-consistent as its slowest replica. A revoke that races a cross-region replication lag surfaces confidential-derived metadata to an unauthorized institution — a contractual and possibly regulatory breach (FERPA/GDPR/export). 'Provably so' is false while this is open.
fix: Make the surface-time gate authoritative, not replica-based, OR bound the replica staleness as a hard fail-closed condition. Concretely: (a) the central index must treat its revocation-set replica's staleness as a security property — if the replica's last-confirmed-sync timestamp exceeds the leak-window SLO, the index must fail CLOSED (refuse to surface that tenant's projections), not serve stale; (b) for high-sensitivity discoverability scopes, require a synchronous freshness token (ZedToken-style) from the grantor cell at surface time rather than trusting the replica; (c) define and SLO revocation-set REPLICATION lag separately from purge lag, with fail-closed behavior when exceeded. State the lease/TTL as the only true backstop and size it to the worst-case replication partition, not to 5s.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
