issue: Embedding publication to the central index is treated as low-risk via '256-d MRL truncation' and 'only public-text embeddings,' but MRL truncation is a dimensionality/quality knob, NOT a privacy mechanism. Truncated embeddings of public text still (a) enable membership inference (which works exist in a tenant's corpus, revealing research direction), (b) support partial text reconstruction via embedding-inversion attacks, and (c) leak co-occurrence/topical structure that, combined with the expertise graph, can deanonymize confidential project participation by inference even though no confidential bytes were published. The threat is named in 11.8 then 'mitigated' by the very thing (truncation) that does not mitigate it.
why: The central index is the aggregated honeypot across all institutions — the highest-value target in the system and operator-readable (control-plane keys, not tenant keys). 'Public-text' embeddings can still reveal that Institution A is building a corpus on a sensitive topic, and inversion research (e.g., vec2text-class attacks) reconstructs substantial text from embeddings regardless of dimension. This undermines the core marketing claim ('confidential never leaves your node, provably') because confidential RESEARCH DIRECTION and project existence leak through the public projection layer.
fix: Either (a) do not publish raw embeddings to the central index at all — publish only opaque, salted, per-tenant-keyed locality-sensitive hashes / quantized codes usable for candidate generation but not invertible, with final scoring brokered to the owning cell; or (b) if embeddings must be published, model inversion explicitly (cite the attack class), apply per-tenant random projection/noise with a stated security parameter, and gate which fields contribute to published embeddings through the same allowlist review. Add membership-inference and inversion to the threat model (11.5) as named, tested controls, not a footnote. Stop describing MRL truncation as the privacy control.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The operator-trust boundary is internally contradictory. The plan claims 'operator cannot see confidential data' and sells 'confidential never leaves your node,' but: (1) BYOK uses Vault that the OPERATOR runs in managed cells ('BYOK via Vault') — the operator holds or can reach the KEK-unwrap path, so 'crypto-shred' and 'university controls custody' are only true for HYOK/sovereign, which is deferred 18 months; (2) the independent egress PEP runs in the data plane the operator operates and inspects PublishableProjections (which can be derived from confidential source) before emission; (3) TEE-backed inference is the stated mitigation for plaintext-at-use but TEE is deferred to 'buyer demand,' so for the entire MVP+Phase-1 window confidential plaintext sits in operator-run cell memory with only swap/coredump disabled. The honest plaintext-at-use admission in 11.1 is good, but the GTM/marketing claims in 1.1/1.3/1.5 ('provably,' 'never leaves') are not qualified to match.
why: For managed cells (the actual MVP and Phase-1 deployment model), the operator is in the TCB for confidential data: holds key-unwrap, runs the egress gate, runs the inference host without a TEE. A CISO doing a real threat model (the named veto-holder persona) will find that 'confidential never leaves your node' collapses to 'confidential never leaves the vendor-operated cell that the vendor can read,' which is a materially weaker and arguably misrepresented claim. This is a sales-integrity and breach-liability problem.
fix: Separate the claim per deployment model explicitly: managed-cell = 'vendor-operated isolation + per-tenant keys + egress gate; vendor is a processor with technical+contractual controls; NOT operator-zero-trust until TEE' ; sovereign/HYOK = 'operator-zero-trust.' Move HYOK key custody and TEE-backed inference EARLIER for any tenant whose threat model is operator-zero-trust (export-controlled labs in the fallback wedge demand exactly this on day one). Do not use the word 'provably' for the managed-cell tier. Add operator-insider to the threat model with the residual-risk statement.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: GDPR right-to-erasure is reconciled solely via crypto-shred, but crypto-shred does not reach two surfaces the plan itself created: (1) the central discovery index holds PublishableProjections + embeddings encrypted under CONTROL-PLANE keys (distinct from tenant KEK, per 4.6) — revoking a tenant KEK does NOT shred control-plane-encrypted central-index data, so an erasure request for a data subject's published projection/profile is not satisfied by crypto-shred and requires an explicit index-purge path that is only described for revocation, not for per-data-subject erasure; (2) federated grantee copies in other institutions' cells are only 'contractually' obligated to shred (4.4 admits this), so a data subject's erasure right cannot be technically guaranteed across the federation. GDPR Art. 17 erasure and Art. 19 notification-to-recipients obligations are not mapped.
why: The plan markets GDPR-processor posture and 'compliance designed in from day one' as a sales weapon. An erasure request that the architecture cannot fully honor (central index + cross-tenant copies) is a direct Art. 17/19 gap. The control-plane-key choice for the central index — made for a good reason (tenant offboarding) — actively breaks the crypto-shred erasure story for personal data in profiles/projections.
fix: Define a per-data-subject erasure workflow distinct from tenant-KEK shred: (a) targeted hard-delete + tombstone of that subject's projections/embeddings in the central index (reuse the revocation tombstone path but keyed by data-subject, not by grant); (b) implement Art. 19 recipient-notification: when a subject is erased, propagate erasure obligations (and ideally technical revoke) to every grantee cell that received a copy, logged in the per-tenant audit; (c) state plainly which erasure guarantees are technical vs contractual, per surface. Map FERPA's analogous correction/access rights too.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The classifier is the single trust root for the entire tier model AND is a probabilistic LLM-adjacent component that is itself a target of prompt injection — yet the two defenses are not composed. 'Fail-closed default = confidential' protects against low-confidence MISSING tiers, but does NOT protect against a confidently-WRONG downgrade: an adversarial document (or a prompt-injection payload embedded in ingested content) that manipulates the classifier into high-confidence 'public' would route confidential content to a cloud frontier model and into the publishable projection. The injection-corpus test only covers exfiltration during synthesis, not classification-time manipulation. Section 8.1 even cites the classifier→route binding as THE kernel differentiator, making this the highest-value attack surface, defended by one probabilistic component.
why: A single high-confidence misclassification is a catastrophic leak: confidential text egresses to a cloud provider (export-control/GDPR/contract breach) and is published to the central honeypot. Fail-closed-on-uncertainty does nothing against confident-wrong, and the plan's own derived-tier MAX rule depends on the source tier being correct in the first place. The independent egress PEP is named as the 'second gate,' but it validates against the allowlist of FIELDS, not against a re-derivation of the SOURCE record's classification — so a record mis-tagged 'public' at ingest sails through the egress PEP cleanly.
fix: Add an independent second classifier (different model/method, e.g., rules + a separate model) and require AGREEMENT for any tier-DOWN decision (public/low-risk routing); disagreement fails closed to confidential. Make the egress PEP re-derive or at least sanity-check classification on emission, not just field-allowlist. Treat ingested document content as untrusted at CLASSIFICATION time (delimiting/structured handling) the same way 8.4 treats it at synthesis time — add classification-time injection to the injection-corpus test. Require human review for any automated public-tier assignment above a corpus-sensitivity threshold for export-controlled/IRB tenants.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: PSI/DP cross-institution privacy budget is per-tenant-PAIR, but the differencing/composition attack across MULTIPLE pairs and across the discovery index is not modeled. An adversary institution can run templated overlap queries against many partners, and separately mine the central discovery index (membership inference per finding 2), then COMPOSE these signals to infer confidential collaboration structure that no single per-pair budget bounds. The k-anonymity floor and per-pair epsilon do not compose to a federation-wide guarantee, and the plan claims DP as a 'required control' without a global accounting model.
why: DP guarantees only hold under correct composition accounting. A per-pair budget with no global ledger means a coalition (or a single well-connected institution) can exceed the intended privacy loss by spreading queries across pairs and combining with index-derived signals — reconstructing exactly the confidential cross-institution relationships the platform exists to protect. This is the classic reason naive per-query/per-pair DP fails in federated settings.
fix: Define a GLOBAL per-subject (and per-confidential-project) privacy ledger that accounts for budget consumed across ALL pairs and across index queries, not per-pair in isolation. Rate-limit and budget at the querying-SUBJECT and target-PROJECT granularity. Explicitly model the composition of (PSI overlap signal + central-index membership signal) as a single adversary capability in the threat model. Until a global accounting model exists, scope PSI to a hard whitelist of pre-approved aggregate questions with conservative fixed thresholds and say so.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: Cross-institution sharing grants carry compliance caveats (transfer_legality_ref, export_attestation_ref, ferpa_institutional_approval) enforced by Cedar at the GRANTOR's PEP, but there is no enforcement that the GRANTEE cell honors the same constraints once data is accessed via zero-copy/brokered query or (where copies are made) replicated. The grant model enforces the egress decision and the read decision at the owner, but the grantee can re-derive, re-embed, re-share downstream, or expose the accessed data to a grantee-side user whose own ABAC attributes (export status, FERPA role) were never checked against the ORIGINATING institution's caveats. There is no notion of a sticky/propagated policy that travels with shared confidential data into the grantee's cell.
why: Confidentiality and regulatory caveats are properties of the DATA, but enforcement is anchored only at the owner. For export-controlled material, the deemed-export rule applies to the PERSON who accesses it — a grantee-side non-US-person could access ITAR data through a valid institution-to-institution grant because the US-person determination was attested by the GRANTOR for the grant, not re-checked against the actual grantee-side accessing individual. Same for FERPA: institutional approval at the grantor does not constrain which grantee-side users see student records.
fix: Make caveats sticky and re-evaluated at the point of grantee-side access: the brokered/zero-copy access path must carry the originating caveats and the owning PEP (or a grantee-side PEP under the grant's policy) must re-check the ACTUAL accessing individual's attributes (US-person, FERPA role) against the grant caveats — not just trust the grantor's institution-level attestation. Define downstream-reshare prohibition as a grant caveat that the egress PEP and SpiceDB enforce on the grantee side (no transitive grants without grantor consent). State which controls are technical vs DUA-contractual.

lens: Maintainability & modularity — module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface/contract clarity, testability, and whether the AI-router and retrieval stay swappable
issue: The ClassificationCapabilityDescriptor is sold as the seam that makes adding a tier or export sub-regime NOT a coordinated multi-contract release ('Router/publish/authz query it rather than switch on it'). This is false decoupling. Tier semantics are not a lookup value — they are pervasive policy: fail-closed default = confidential, derived artifacts inherit MAX(input tiers), output clamped to highest tier present, BYO selectable only for public/low-risk, confidential routes local-only, PLG cells structurally cannot host confidential. Every one of those is a branch on tier identity, not a data-driven query. Adding a fourth tier (e.g. 'export-controlled' as distinct from confidential) forces edits to IClassifier, the MAX-tier lattice in derivation, the router precedence table, the egress allowlist review, the Cedar narrow-only rules, and the edition-posture derivation — i.e. the entire policy kernel plus the serving kernel's router. The descriptor indirection hides where the lattice and ordering live, making the coordinated change harder to find, not unnecessary.
why: The plan's central maintainability claim for the most volatile dimension (data classification, which WILL change as new compliance regimes appear) is that blast radius is contained behind one descriptor. In reality the tier lattice is the most cross-cutting concept in the system. A team that believes the descriptor decouples them will add a tier as a 'config change,' miss the MAX-lattice and clamp ordering, and ship a confidentiality leak — the exact failure class the plan calls existential.
fix: Drop the 'query not switch' framing. Instead specify the tier lattice as a single owned module (a partial order with explicit join/MAX, a declared default, and a routing/clamp policy table) that the policy kernel imports, and make 'add a tier' an explicit, checklisted, test-gated policy-kernel release with a fixture that exercises every consumer (router, derivation, egress, Cedar, edition derivation). Have the import-linter and a dedicated exhaustiveness test (e.g. a typed enum/total-match check) FAIL the build when a new tier is added without updating each consumer, turning the cross-cutting change into a compiler-enforced fan-out rather than a silently-incomplete config edit.

lens: Maintainability & modularity — module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface/contract clarity, testability, and whether the AI-router and retrieval stay swappable
issue: IExpertiseGraph is the load-bearing decoupling mechanism for the module DAG (it is how mod-funding avoids importing mod-discovery), yet it is never specified and is structurally overloaded. Section 5.3 says mod-discovery 'publishes' to it, mod-workspaces and mod-funding 'consume' it, and it is simultaneously 'kernel-owned.' A contract that one module writes, two modules read, and the kernel owns, carrying 'scholarly graph + expertise fingerprints,' is a shared canonical model — the classic god-contract. Its schema will accrete every field any pillar needs (collaborator edges for discovery, expertise vectors for funding team-assembly, membership for workspaces), and because all four pillars couple to it, every change to it has cross-pillar blast radius. The DAG is acyclic on paper only because the cycle was relocated into an unspecified shared schema.
why: This is precisely where v1 rotted (mod-funding -> mod-discovery). v2 renamed the edge instead of removing the coupling. As pillars evolve, IExpertiseGraph becomes the bottleneck contract: you cannot add a discovery feature without touching the schema funding/workspaces depend on, so the 'add a feature with minimal blast radius' requirement fails for any feature that touches expertise/collaboration — which is most of pillars 1, 3, and 4.
fix: Specify IExpertiseGraph now as a versioned, additive-only, READ-only projection with a named owner (kernel, not mod-discovery — clarify that mod-discovery emits raw events the kernel projects, so mod-discovery does NOT 'publish the contract'). Split it if it carries more than one bounded concern: e.g. IExpertiseFingerprint (vectors, consumed by funding/discovery) vs ICollaborationGraph (edges/membership, consumed by workspaces/discovery). Add a consumer registry entry per field so a field can be retired when no pillar reads it. Without a concrete schema and ownership rule, this is the one contract guaranteed to rot.

lens: Maintainability & modularity — module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface/contract clarity, testability, and whether the AI-router and retrieval stay swappable
issue: The PublishableProjection design creates a tight compile-time/CI coupling between every internal entity's data model and the central-index consumer, with no evolution story. The 'total projection function where every field defaults to non-published' plus 'any new internal-entity field is non-publishable unless added to a PublishableProjection' means the projection schemas are a second, parallel data model that must be maintained in lockstep with the internal entities AND with whatever the central index and Discovery PEP expect. The plan specifies the safety property (nothing leaks) but says nothing about how a projection schema versions, how the central index handles nodes emitting older/newer projection versions (Section 10.2 mentions schema-registry compat for events generically, but projection field-allowlist changes are a distinct, security-sensitive schema), or how adding a publishable field is reviewed and rolled out across self-hosted nodes on independent upgrade cycles.
why: This is the federation seam — the existential surface. A maintainability gap here is also a security gap. If a new publishable field is added centrally but an older node doesn't emit it (or vice versa), you get either silent discovery degradation or, worse, a node emitting a field the central allowlist no longer recognizes and the egress PEP/consumer disagreeing about what is permitted. 'Minimal blast radius' for adding a discoverable field is unaddressed precisely where the radius is widest (every node + index + Discovery PEP).
fix: Specify PublishableProjection as a first-class versioned contract in shared/contracts with its own additive-only evolution rule, a per-field 'introduced-in-version' and classification-review record, and a forward/backward-compat requirement that the central index accepts the intersection of fields across the version range currently deployed across nodes. Make the egress PEP validate against the node's emitted projection version, not a single global allowlist. Add a contract test that a node at version N and index at version M agree on the allowlist intersection.

lens: Maintainability & modularity — module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface/contract clarity, testability, and whether the AI-router and retrieval stay swappable
issue: The entire modularity guarantee rests on the import-linter CI gate, but enforcement at the import-graph level does not stop the most common real decoupling failures: communication through shared data stores and through the kernel read-models. import-linter forbids module->module Python imports and concrete-store imports outside kernel adapters. It cannot detect mod-funding reading rows that mod-discovery wrote into a shared table, two modules coordinating via agreed-upon keys in the same Postgres/OpenSearch index, or a module depending on undocumented emergent fields/semantics of IExpertiseGraph. Given the MVP explicitly CONSOLIDATES everything into one Postgres (pgvector+AGE+tsvector+audit+outbox), the physical substrate actively invites cross-module data coupling that the import gate is blind to.
why: The plan repeatedly calls the boundaries 'machine-verified' and treats the import gate as proof of modularity. Teams will trust the green check and couple through the shared database, which is the most insidious form of coupling because it survives all import checks and only surfaces when one module's write breaks another's read. Consolidating to one store for operational simplicity directly trades away the schema-level isolation that would otherwise back the import-level claim.
fix: Add per-module schema/namespace ownership inside the consolidated Postgres (separate schemas with grants so module A's DB role cannot read module B's tables) and a CI/migration check that no module migration references another module's schema. State explicitly that cross-module data access is ONLY via kernel read-models/events, and enforce it at the DB-permission layer, not just the import layer. Otherwise narrow the modularity claim to 'import-level decoupling' and stop calling boundaries machine-verified.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The entire revenue plan rests on a wedge buyer the plan itself cannot confirm exists. The MVP, beachhead, pricing, network-effect bootstrap, and SOC2-bridge ALL depend on landing 'a pre-bonded 2-node consortium with an executable master agreement, two members who will co-deploy at the same time, AND a security line-item budget of $2.5–8k/mo/node.' That is a four-way AND across legal readiness, technical readiness, joint scheduling, and budget — and the plan files the existence of even ONE such buyer under Open Question #1 ('spike: outreach to 5–8 candidates'). The architecture decisions are downstream of a buyer that has not been validated to exist.
why: If this specific compound buyer cannot be found and closed within runway, the company has no first revenue and no de-risked federation seam — both of which v2 explicitly sequences FIRST. Existing master agreements (NSF/NIH center DUAs, CTSA, Big Ten Academic Alliance) almost never pre-authorize ingesting confidential data into a third-party SaaS vendor's cells; they govern data sharing between the institutions, not with a new processor. So even a 'pre-bonded' dyad likely still triggers a fresh DPA/security review for THIS vendor — collapsing the central premise that the master agreement 'short-circuits fresh DUA.'
fix: Before committing the architecture sequence, run the buyer-discovery spike FIRST and gate the plan on it: name 3 concrete candidate dyads, confirm in writing that their existing master agreement permits onboarding a new SaaS processor without fresh full security review, and get a verbal price indication. If none clears, the wedge must change BEFORE engineering, not after. Treat 'master agreement covers a new processor' as a falsifiable assumption to test in week 1, not a stated fact in Section 2.2.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Differentiated value and fast first revenue are in direct, unacknowledged tension. The plan's moat IS confidential cross-institution data sharing — which is exactly the capability that triggers the maximum procurement friction: IT security review (HECVAT 321 questions), CISO sign-off, DPA negotiation, possibly IRB, and for the fallback export lab, ITAR/DCSA. v2 correctly retired the fast-but-commodity public wedge, but in doing so it moved first revenue onto the single slowest-to-sell capability in the catalog, then claims first revenue arrives fast because the dyad shows value with 'zero confidential sharing needed' first.
why: The 'zero confidential sharing' demo (public-tier federated discovery between two institutions) is precisely the capability that VIVO/Direct2Experts federates for FREE across 64 institutions. So the fast/early demonstrable slice has no paid differentiation, and the paid differentiation (confidential workspace + revocable grant) is gated behind the full security/legal review the plan elsewhere admits takes 12–18 months. The revenue-bearing increment and the fast-to-show increment are different increments — the plan conflates them to manufacture a 'fast differentiated wedge' that doesn't exist as a single sellable motion.
fix: Be explicit about which increment the design partner PAYS for at first dollar and when that payment clears the buyer's review gate. If the paid trigger is the confidential workspace, model the real first-dollar date as post-security-review (likely 6–12 months from first contact) and size runway to it. If you want genuinely fast first revenue, you may need a paid pilot/POC contract (services-style, not SaaS subscription) as the actual Phase-0 revenue instrument — say so, because that changes the revenue quality and the funding story.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The fallback wedge (single export-controlled / defense lab) is presented as the lower-risk safety net but is almost certainly a HARDER first sale than the primary wedge, and the plan under-prices that. An export-controlled lab buying a SaaS vendor for ITAR/EAR-relevant data implies CUI handling, likely CMMC 2.0 / NIST 800-171 compliance, DCSA scrutiny, US-person personnel controls on the VENDOR side, and a strong on-prem/air-gap expectation — yet the plan defers sovereign/on-prem out of the first 18 months and offers a 'managed cell.' A defense lab will not put ITAR data in a pre-revenue startup's managed cloud cell.
why: The fallback is supposed to de-risk the case where the dyad doesn't close. But it swaps a hard sale for a harder one with a longer compliance moat (CMMC alone is a multi-quarter, capital-intensive certification) and a deployment posture (sovereign/on-prem) the plan explicitly defers. So the 'fallback' is not actually available within the MVP architecture and timeline — it's a different, more expensive company.
fix: Either (a) drop the export-lab fallback as a Phase-0 option and find a genuinely easier fallback (e.g., a single well-funded R1 center buying the confidential single-tenant product for internal-only use, no export controls, no federation, priced on the security guarantee), or (b) if you keep the export-lab path, honestly state it requires the sovereign/on-prem appliance + CMMC posture and therefore is NOT a Phase-0 fallback but a Phase-2+ vertical with its own capital requirement.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Willingness-to-pay at $2.5–8k/mo/node is asserted as 'defensible' but is anchored only to internal COGS, not to any external evidence of what a research office or center will actually pay — and the plan's own competitive table shows the comparable confidential-collaboration value (VIVO federation) is free and the AI-grant fast-follow (Atom) is $179/mo across 50+ institutions. The plan derives price bottom-up from confidential-GPU COGS ('price the security guarantee'), but buyers don't pay your COGS; they pay their perceived value ceiling, which the same plan shows is depressed by free/cheap incumbents.
why: Pricing on COGS in a market with free/cheap adjacent substitutes is how startups discover their floor price exceeds the market's ceiling price — i.e., the unit economics are still inverted, just at a different price point. A research center on a fixed indirect-cost recovery budget paying $30–100k/yr/node for a security guarantee they currently get via DUA + their own infra for ~zero marginal cost is a hard, unevidenced sell. The 'security line-item budget' is assumed to exist and absorb this; that is the actual risk and it's listed as Open Question #2, downstream of the architecture commitment.
fix: Pressure-test value-based (not cost-based) WTP before building: run 8–10 pricing interviews with RD officers/CISOs framed as 'what would you pay to share confidential project data with a partner institution without it leaving your boundary, replacing your current DUA+manual process?' If the answer clusters below the COGS floor, the confidential-managed-cell model is dead on arrival and you must shift COGS to the buyer (BYO-compute) as the DEFAULT, not the PLG-only afterthought it currently is. Also model gross margin at the floor price including the GPU — a 'few hundred to ~$1k+/mo GPU alone' against a $2.5k floor is a thin-to-negative margin once you add support, SOC2 amortization, and CILogon.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The network-effect cold-start is not actually solved — it's restated. The moat is 'value compounds per connected node; worthless single-tenant, defensible at N≥2.' The bootstrap mechanism is 'seed a pre-bonded dyad.' But a dyad is N=2 with exactly ONE possible cross-institution edge, and the value of that edge to the two members is bounded by what they already get from co-authoring directly. There is no mechanism described for getting from N=2 (one design-partner dyad) to N≥10 where cross-institution discovery has real combinatorial value — and each new node requires its own institutional security review + DPA, so node acquisition does not get cheaper as N grows (no supply-side or demand-side economy of scale in onboarding).
why: Classic two-sided/network products bootstrap by making single-side value standalone-useful (so the first nodes join without the network) and then letting cross-side value compound. Here the plan admits single-tenant is 'worthless' for the moat, and cross-node value only exists at N≥2 within an ALREADY-bonded relationship — meaning the network never bootstraps beyond pre-existing collaboration clusters the platform didn't create. The platform captures value from collaborations that already exist; it doesn't manufacture new cross-institution value, which is what the funding narrative ('federation network effect') promises.
fix: Specify the concrete path from the first dyad to a self-reinforcing network: which side is standalone-useful enough to acquire nodes without the network (likely the intra-institution research-intelligence product, which must be independently worth buying), and what specifically makes node #11's join attractive to nodes #1–10. If the honest answer is 'we ride existing consortia (CTSA, Big Ten),' then your TAM is bounded by existing consortium membership and your GTM is consortium-BD, not PLG-bootstrapped network growth — which contradicts the 'PLG funnel as brand surface / expansion' story and should be reconciled.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The PLG tier — the only motion that could cheaply bootstrap bottom-up adoption and brand — has been scoped down to 'public literature + the researcher's own draft materials, AI Q&A with no training on your data,' which the plan itself calls a 'modest' increment 'priced accordingly.' Against free/generous-tier Elicit, Consensus, SciSpace, NotebookLM, and ChatGPT-with-files, this PLG product has no defensible differentiation and no obvious reason a researcher pays for it.
why: v2 correctly de-fanged the CISO-shadow-IT risk by removing confidential data from PLG. But in doing so it gutted PLG's value prop to something the market already gives away. So PLG cannot serve its stated Phase-2 role as an 'expansion funnel and brand surface' — a funnel that no one enters because the free product is as good is not a funnel. The locked requirement that one product serve the bottom-up PLG buyer is technically satisfied (packaging exists) but commercially hollow.
fix: Either (a) accept PLG is a loss-leader / brand-awareness play and stop modeling it as revenue or a meaningful funnel, and reallocate that GTM energy to the consortium/institution motion; or (b) find a PLG increment that is genuinely differentiated AND safe — e.g., grounded Q&A over the researcher's OWN institutional library/subscription corpus via a sanctioned library connector (a real gap vs Elicit), which requires institutional buy-in and thus is really a land-and-expand into the top-down motion, not true self-serve PLG. Be honest about which it is.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Sales-cycle-vs-runway is never quantified, and the plan's own facts imply it is fatal. SOC2 Type II gates the first institutional contract and takes a 6–12 month observation window 'once controls are stable' (i.e., not from day 1). HECVAT, DPA negotiation, and multi-institution legal review for confidential federation add months. The plan acknowledges a '12–18 month institutional cycle' and says the consortium master-agreement wedge 'bridges' it — but the wedge revenue is itself gated behind security review (Finding #2), and a 5-person team with a fractional GTM hire running ONE motion cannot parallelize. There is no stated runway, burn, or revenue-by-month model anywhere in Section 16/18.
why: A from-scratch startup selling confidential, multi-party, security-reviewed software into universities and consortia is signing up for the longest enterprise sales cycles that exist, with the smallest possible sales org (one fractional hire). If first real (SaaS, post-review) revenue is 12–18 months out and Phase-0 'first paying design-partner node' is really a paid pilot, the company needs 24–30 months of runway and a funding story that explicitly accounts for a long pre-revenue valley. The cost section lists COGS lines but no burn, no runway, no break-even customer count realized against a timeline.
fix: Add a burn/runway/revenue-by-quarter model. State the assumed seed raise, monthly burn for 5 FTE + cloud staging + SOC2 (~$40–70k/yr) + GPU + CILogon, and overlay the realistic first-dollar and first-recurring-revenue dates given the security/legal gates. If the model shows recurring revenue arriving after month 15+, either the team must shrink, the wedge must get faster (paid pilots/services), or the raise must be larger — make that explicit rather than implying revenue 'first.'

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: Phase-0 MVP is still too large for 3 engineers despite the 'reduced kernel' framing. The Phase-0 build list contains ~12 distinct hard subsystems: fail-closed classification, pure model router, ITransportPolicy egress block, IPolicyEnforcement-over-RBAC, hash-chained audit, PublishableProjection structural type + total projection function, independent egress PEP, continuous runtime leak assertion, deterministic identity resolution, a full composable retrieval pipeline (retriever/fuser/reranker) with RAGAS-in-CI, a functional confidential tier on vLLM, AND live 2-node public-tier federation with one revocable cell-local-SpiceDB grant — plus standing up cloud staging and a SOC2 Type I sprint. Each of the security-critical pieces (egress PEP correctness, projection type, cell-local grant semantics, runtime leak assertion) is a multi-week hardening effort on its own because a single bug is the existential leak the whole product sells against.
why: This is the exact failure mode the plan claims to have fixed in v1 ('16-month death march for 3 engineers'). Two-cell live federation + a confidential egress gate that must be provably correct is not an MVP — it is the full moat. Building it before a single paying customer is validated means burning the entire runway on infrastructure for a design partner that section 19 admits has not been identified yet.
fix: Cut Phase-0 to a single-cell functional confidential product (no live second cell, no central index, no federation seam) for the FALLBACK buyer (one export-controlled or security-sensitive lab). Ship: classification + router + transport-policy egress block + confidential local inference + hybrid retrieval + audit. Defer the egress PEP / PublishableProjection / runtime leak assertion / 2-node seam to Phase-1, because they only earn their cost once a SECOND node exists. Make the consortium dyad Phase-1, not Phase-0. This halves the build and lets first revenue land on N=1 differentiated security value while the dyad is being sourced.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: The entire revenue plan is gated on finding a rare, specific artifact: a pre-bonded 2-node consortium with an EXECUTABLE master agreement AND two members willing to co-deploy a brand-new vendor's GPU cells. Section 19 Q1 admits this partner is not yet identified and the discovery method is 'outreach to 5-8 candidates.' The fallback (single export-controlled lab) front-loads ITAR/EAR US-person attestation, FRE-loss reasoning, and the highest-ACV/highest-friction procurement — the opposite of an easy first sale.
why: A founder-feasibility plan cannot have its first dollar depend on an external precondition with an unknown hit rate and a months-long sales cycle. If no dyad closes in the first 2 quarters, the company has built two-cell federation infrastructure with zero revenue and a fallback buyer whose compliance bar is the most expensive in the catalog. This is a single point of go-to-market failure dressed up as 'resolved.'
fix: De-risk by making the first sellable unit independent of the dyad: a single-tenant confidential research-intelligence cell (the fallback's product, minus the ITAR gate) sold to ANY security-sensitive lab or funded center — not requiring export controls, not requiring a second institution. Validate willingness-to-pay at the $2.5-8k/mo band against 5-8 such single buyers BEFORE committing engineering to the federation seam. Treat the consortium dyad as the expansion proof, not the survival-critical first close.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: Per-tenant key isolation depends on Vault namespaces, which are Enterprise-only (low-six-figures/yr, unpublished pricing, +25-60% hidden cost per vendor reports). The plan lists this as Open Question #6 ('confirm OSS suffices... else budget Enterprise') but the answer is already knowable: Vault Community lacks namespaces. The per-tenant KMS/namespace isolation story — load-bearing for BYOK/HYOK and crypto-shred — likely forces Vault Enterprise or a re-architecture onto per-cell separate Vault instances / cloud KMS.
why: This is a hard COGS line the cost model (Section 16) does not include. A low-six-figure annual licensing floor materially changes breakeven customer count, and the M4 Max / OSS-first ethos in the project's own constraints (cloud APIs opt-in, default to local/free) is violated silently. Discovering this post-commit is a budget shock.
fix: Resolve now, not as an open question: design per-tenant key isolation on per-cell isolated Vault Community instances (one Vault per cell, naturally tenant-isolated, no namespaces needed) OR cloud KMS per-tenant keys. Both avoid the Enterprise license. State explicitly that Vault namespaces are NOT used, and add whichever path you pick to the COGS table with a dollar figure.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: Cloud-GPU COGS is understated. The plan's 'realistic floor ≈ a few hundred to ~$1k+/mo per dedicated cell in cloud GPU alone' covers only one always-on small GPU. Verified pricing: a single always-on A10G (g5) or L4 (g6) is ~$735-1,454/mo for the instance ALONE. A production confidential cell also needs the vLLM host headroom, the consolidated Postgres (pgvector+AGE+tsvector) compute/RAM, object storage, and any redundancy. There is no HA/failover GPU budgeted, yet Section 14.4 promises 99.9% cell availability — a single non-redundant GPU instance cannot hit 99.9% through a host failure or AZ event.
why: Realistic all-in confidential-cell COGS is likely $1.5-3k/mo, not 'a few hundred,' which compresses margin at the $2.5-8k/mo price band far more than implied — especially for the smaller end of that range. And the 99.9% SLO is unfundable on a single GPU without a standby, which is another unbudgeted GPU.
fix: Rebuild the per-cell COGS as a line-item stack (GPU instance + standby-or-degraded-mode policy + Postgres node + storage + egress + share of central index + monitoring) and state the all-in monthly figure. Either budget a warm-standby GPU (doubling GPU cost) or explicitly downgrade the confidential-cell availability SLO to single-instance reality (e.g. 99.5% with a documented recovery procedure). Do not promise 99.9% on non-redundant GPU.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: The central sharded OpenSearch discovery index is correctly reclassified as first-class stateful (good), and sized in GB (30-150GB), but never sized in DOLLARS or assigned an owner/ops model. A continuously-CDC-fed, multi-region, encrypted, authz-gated, scatter-gather OpenSearch cluster with separate write/query node pools and per-tenant quotas is a meaningful standing cost AND a meaningful ops burden (cluster upgrades, shard rebalancing, hot-tenant tuning) that lands on the same 3-person team.
why: This is shared infrastructure whose cost is NOT covered by any single tenant's per-node fee, so it is pure overhead until enough nodes amortize it — and it arrives in Phase 2 right when the team is also running multiple live cells. An unsized shared-overhead component plus an OpenSearch cluster to operate is a classic small-team ops sink.
fix: Add a dollar line for the central index at N=10/50/200 to the cost model, and decide explicitly between self-managed OpenSearch vs a managed service (OpenSearch Serverless / managed Qdrant Cloud) — managed shifts ops burden off the 3 engineers at higher unit cost, which is the right trade pre-scale. Tie central-index cost recovery to the consortium/exchange add-on pricing so it is not silent overhead.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility — buildability by a small team, infra cost shape, self-host burden per node, dev complexity vs MVP, over-engineering, licensing traps, M4 Max dev-vs-prod gap
issue: The four-buyer 'one product, sequenced motions' framing still implies the product must SUPPORT all four packaging editions (Lab/Department/Institution/Consortium/Sovereign) from a constant architecture — including a shared soft-isolated PLG cell with per-tenant GPU/RAM quotas and fair scheduling (Section 14.5), edition-config tier-availability derivation, and a sovereign single-appliance image. Even if only one motion is SOLD at a time, the architectural surface for all editions is asserted as constant and therefore must be designed (and largely built) up front to avoid rework.
why: Designing five isolation postures, a multi-tenant fair-scheduled PLG substrate, AND a self-contained sovereign appliance image into a from-scratch platform is a large design tax for a team that will only sell one edition for 18+ months. The locked requirement is 'one product via packaging,' but nothing forces all packaging variants to be built early — the plan blurs 'architecture stays constant' into 'build the variation points now.'
fix: Explicitly state which edition variation points are DESIGNED-only (interfaces reserved, not implemented) vs BUILT in each phase. The PLG shared-cell scheduler, sovereign appliance image, and BYO-compute path should be design-reserved seams with zero implementation until their phase. Confirm in writing that the single-cell managed edition is the only fully-built deployment topology through Phase 1.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation topology, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, knowledge-graph + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The freshness watermark — the single load-bearing correctness primitive — has no specified propagation mechanism, no monotonicity-under-failover semantics, and no end-to-end latency budget; its feasibility is punted to Open Question #4. The plan repeatedly says 'consumer checks its revocation freshness watermark' and 'fail closed if older than (now − leak-window)' but never specifies HOW a watermark gets from the single-region linearizable authority to (a) every cell PEP and (b) every shard of the managed central index within the <5s SLO. The path is: authority assigns version → emits 'monotonic version + freshness watermark feed' → cells AND index consume → per-shard bitmap materializes → query filters on it. That is a multi-hop pipeline (authority → CDC/feed → per-shard refresh) whose total latency must be < 5s at p99 under load, across regions, with publish-shedding active. Nowhere is this budget summed the way 14.4.1 sums the query path. Worse: 'now' in '(now − leak-window)' is a clock comparison, and the trust model explicitly says no node's self-asserted clock orders security decisions — but the freshness gate compares the watermark's authority-version-or-timestamp against the CONSUMER'S local 'now'. If the consumer's clock is skewed forward, it fails closed prematurely (availability hit); if skewed backward, it serves stale data past the leak-window (the exact leak the gate exists to prevent). The watermark must be a signed authority-timestamp compared against a bounded-skew local clock, and that bound must be inside the leak-window — none of which is specified.
why: Every confidentiality, compliance (GDPR erasure within the window, deemed-export revocation, subject deprovisioning), and revocation claim in the plan reduces to 'the freshness gate works within the leak-window.' If the propagation pipeline cannot actually hit <5s p99 — or if the clock-comparison is unsound — then the headline invariant ('fail-closed when the freshness watermark is older than the leak-window') is either an availability disaster (over-firing) or a silent leak (under-firing). This is the spine of the product and it is asserted, not engineered.
fix: Specify the watermark as a signed (authority_monotonic_version, authority_wallclock) tuple. Define the freshness check as: max accepted authority_version seen by this consumer, AND authority_wallclock newer than (consumer_clock − leak_window − max_clock_skew_bound), where max_clock_skew_bound is enforced (NTP/PTP discipline with alarms; consumers whose skew exceeds the bound fail closed). Sum the propagation budget explicitly the way 14.4.1 sums the query path: authority commit → feed publish → per-shard/per-cell apply → queryable, at p99, under publish-shedding. State the dependency: the leak-window SLO is achievable ONLY if propagation p99 + clock-skew-bound < leak-window; if it isn't, set the leak-window honestly (e.g., 30s) rather than asserting 5s. Move this from Open Question #4 to a designed mechanism with a contract test.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation topology, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, knowledge-graph + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The single-region linearizable Revocation Authority is a global synchronous dependency on the security-critical path with no HA, failover, or self-partition story — and its own unavailability is conflated with 'control-plane partition' without analyzing the difference. Section 12 lists it as 'Single-region linearizable store (e.g. etcd/Spanner-class).' Single-region etcd is a 3-5 node Raft group in ONE region: a region outage takes it down entirely. When the authority is DOWN (not partitioned-from — actually down), it cannot advance the watermark, so EVERY cell's watermark ages past the leak-window and EVERY confidential cross-tenant drill-down across the ENTIRE platform fails closed simultaneously. That is a global blast radius from a single-region component, which directly contradicts the 'failure domains are cell-isolated' claim (14.3) and the 99.95% control-plane SLO (a single-region Raft store does not get 99.95% through region failures). The plan also never specifies: how a new version is assigned during authority leader election (gap in monotonicity?), what happens to in-flight revokes during failover, or whether the authority itself can be partitioned from the CDC feed it must drive.
why: A 'single-region linearizable authority' that orders every security decision is the textbook single point of failure the rest of the architecture works hard to avoid. Its downtime degrades the entire federated product to deny — turning a routine cloud-region blip into a platform-wide confidential-access outage. For a product whose pitch is 'sovereign, resilient, your data stays available in your cell,' a global synchronous control dependency that can dark the cross-institution surface platform-wide is a contradiction of the deployment promise.
fix: Either (a) make the authority multi-region with a defined consensus (Spanner/CockroachDB with explicit replica placement and a stated commit-latency cost on the revoke write path — note PSI's ledger already accepts one such dependency, so reuse the analysis), accepting higher revoke-commit latency, OR (b) explicitly bound the blast radius: define authority-unavailability as distinct from partition, give cells a 'last-known-good watermark + grace policy' that is itself a security decision (e.g., revoke writes are buffered durably and the leak-window clock is measured from authority recovery, with the outage surfaced as degraded), and state the worst-case global-deny exposure. Specify version monotonicity across leader elections (fenced/lease-based version allocation, no gaps, no reuse). Add a failure-injection test: kill the authority and assert the platform degrades to deny without violating monotonicity on recovery.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation topology, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, knowledge-graph + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The two-tier topical index's 'few shards per query' fan-out claim is unverified hand-waving, and the topical (embedding-cluster) shard key creates a re-clustering / drift problem the plan never addresses. The plan asserts that sharding by embedding cluster means 'the dominant query hits FEW shards' and prevents a mega-shard from dominating p95. But: (1) research topics are power-law distributed — a few hot topics (ML, genomics, cancer) will concentrate a huge fraction of corpus AND queries into a few topical shards, recreating the exact hot-shard problem 'topic-subsharding mega-R1s' was supposed to solve, except now the hotspot is cross-tenant and unavoidable. (2) Embedding clusters are not static: as the corpus grows and as embedding models are upgraded (the plan explicitly upgrades nomic→Qwen3 and contemplates model evolution), cluster boundaries drift, forcing re-sharding/re-clustering of a multi-tenant managed index — a massive, ongoing, hot-path-affecting operation that is never costed. (3) A genuinely cross-cutting query ('who works on X across the consortium') where X spans multiple clusters still scatters; the 'few shards' claim holds only for tightly-clustered queries. (4) The per-shard revocation bitmap must be replicated to EVERY shard a tenant's records land in — with topical sharding, a single tenant's records are smeared across many topical shards, so the bitmap is not one-per-tenant-cell but fanned across shards, multiplying the watermark-refresh write load the plan claims to have minimized.
why: Sharding strategy is the core scalability decision for the central index, and topical sharding was chosen specifically to fix tenant-sharding's fan-out. But it trades a known, bounded problem (tenant skew, fixable by subsharding) for an unbounded, dynamic one (topical hotspots + embedding drift + smeared revocation bitmaps). If hot topical shards dominate p95 or re-clustering becomes a recurring fire, the 800ms discovery SLO and the managed-ops-off-3-engineers premise both break.
fix: Cost the topical-skew distribution explicitly on synthetic OpenAlex data (Open Q #7 mentions fan-out but not skew or drift): measure query and corpus concentration across clusters, not just average fan-out. Specify the re-clustering/re-embedding migration as a first-class operation (versioned embedding space, dual-index during cutover, who pays, downtime), the same way 6.3 specifies the intra-cell split. Quantify the per-shard revocation-bitmap write amplification given that a tenant's records smear across N topical shards — reconcile this against the DS-high-lease-write-amp resolution which assumed bitmap refresh was cheap. Consider a hybrid: topical macro-shard + per-tenant sub-partition so a tenant's revocation bitmap stays local to a bounded shard set.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation topology, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, knowledge-graph + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The CDC / event-sync mechanics carry the tombstone-ordering and freshness-propagation guarantees, but the plan's sync stack is internally inconsistent across phases and the exactly-once-effect claim is overstated. Phase 0/1 use 'transactional-outbox polling'; Debezium+Kafka CDC arrives only at Phase 2 'when the central index first exists.' But the central index is ALSO Phase 2, and the freshness watermark + tombstone feed must reach BOTH cells AND the index. So in Phase 1 (first federation, first revocable grant, first freshness gate — per 3.5), there is NO Kafka and NO central index, yet the plan claims the authority 'emits a CDC tombstone' and feeds a watermark to consumers. What is the Phase-1 transport for the watermark/tombstone feed across cells if CDC doesn't exist until Phase 2? Outbox-polling has a polling interval that directly bounds the achievable leak-window — a 1s poll interval alone eats 20% of a 5s budget, and polling does not naturally fan out a single authority watermark to many consumers. Separately, 'exactly-once effect on at-least-once delivery via authority-version + idempotency keys' is correct for idempotent upserts/deletes but the plan also runs side-effecting consumers (notifications, key-rotation via Temporal, audit-receipt exchange) where idempotency keys do not make the EFFECT exactly-once for free — that requires per-consumer dedup state that is asserted but not designed.
why: The revocation correctness story assumes a tombstone-and-watermark distribution substrate. If that substrate is outbox-polling in the exact phase the freshness gate first ships (Phase 1), the polling interval is an unacknowledged additive term in the leak-window budget, and the fan-out of one authority watermark to many cells over polling is unspecified. A guarantee that depends on a transport that doesn't exist yet in the phase it's promised is a phase-sequencing hole in the most important mechanism.
fix: Specify the Phase-1 watermark/tombstone transport explicitly (a dedicated authority→cell push/long-poll feed independent of the Phase-2 Kafka CDC, since the central index isn't the only consumer — cells need it too). Add the polling/feed interval as an explicit line item in the leak-window budget (per the crit-1 recommendation). Distinguish idempotent-effect consumers (index upsert/delete — fine) from genuinely side-effecting consumers (notifications, key-rotation) and specify the dedup/once-effect mechanism for the latter, or state they are at-least-once with idempotent side effects designed in. Reconcile the Phase-1-no-Kafka / Phase-1-needs-watermark-feed contradiction in the 3.5 table.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation topology, control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, knowledge-graph + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: Brokered drill-down availability is computed as ~98.5% (product of A-PEP × token-mint × B-cell), the consequence is acknowledged in one line ('degrade to publishable-metadata-only'), but the product/scale implication is never confronted: at the platform's core value action, ~1.5% of cross-institution detail requests fail, and this compounds with the freshness-gate deny and the authority-availability deny into a much worse effective number. The plan treats degradation as benign UX ('detail unavailable'). But the entire premise of the federated tier is rich cross-institution discovery + drill-down. Three independent deny sources stack on the SAME request: (1) brokered-path component product ~98.5%, (2) freshness watermark stale-beyond-leak-window → deny, (3) authority unavailable → deny (crit-2). These are not independent in a correlated outage (a control-plane region blip hits token mint AND the authority AND the watermark feed simultaneously), so the real-world failure of cross-institution drill-down during any control-plane degradation approaches 100%, not 1.5%. The cross-region path (14.4.1) also omits the freshness-check round-trip latency entirely from its sum, and ignores tail amplification across the 6 serial stages (p95 of a 6-stage serial pipeline is NOT the sum of per-stage p95s — it's worse).
why: The cross-institution drill-down is THE differentiated action the whole architecture exists to enable. If it is the least-available, most-deny-prone path in the system — and degrades to 'metadata only' precisely when a partner institution most wants the detail — the product's headline capability is its flakiest. Underselling this as a one-line UX note hides a real reliability ceiling that buyers' CISOs and end users will hit.
fix: Model the COMPOUND availability of confidential cross-institution drill-down with correlated control-plane failure (token mint + authority + watermark feed share a failure domain). State the honest effective availability during a control-plane degradation (likely near-total deny, not 98.5%). Add the freshness-check latency to the 14.4.1 cross-region sum, and replace the additive p95 with a proper serial-pipeline tail estimate (or a measured end-to-end SLO). Decide whether 'metadata-only during degradation' is acceptable for the beachhead use case or whether some confidential reads need a cell-local cached-grant + last-good-watermark grace path (which reopens the partition tradeoff — make the call).

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: The freshness-watermark gate is fail-OPEN inside the leak-window, and the leak-window applies to the WRONG event. The plan's invariant is: surface/drill-down is denied only when the watermark is older than (now − leak-window). But a revocation that was committed to the authority 1 second ago is, by construction, INSIDE the leak-window — so a cell whose watermark is fresh (e.g. 2s old) will happily serve data that was revoked 1s ago, because the watermark says 'I am fresh' while not yet carrying the new tombstone. Freshness of the watermark is not the same as having-seen-the-revocation. The gate proves 'my replication channel is alive,' NOT 'this specific grant is not revoked.' The plan conflates liveness-of-channel with currency-of-decision. So the actual exposure window for any single revocation is the full propagation latency (commit-at-authority → tombstone reaches this cell/shard), which is exactly the leak-window the plan claims to bound — but it is served as ALLOW, not deny, the whole time. The fail-closed only triggers on TOTAL channel staleness (partition), not on per-record propagation delay.
why: This is the central confidentiality claim of the entire platform. The headline invariant says revocation is 'genuinely correct under partition' and 'fail-closed when the freshness watermark is older than the leak-window.' But the dangerous case is not partition — it is the normal-operation propagation delay of a single fresh revocation, during which the gate is fail-OPEN. An attacker (or a deprovisioned insider, or a partner whose grant was just pulled because of an IP dispute) retains access for the full propagation window on a channel that reports itself healthy. The plan markets a deny-by-default revocation gate that is actually allow-by-default for the first leak-window seconds after every revocation. For confidential research IP and ITAR-adjacent data, 'we leak for up to 5 seconds after every revoke, by design, on a healthy channel' is a materially different (and weaker) guarantee than what every customer-facing sentence in the doc states.
fix: Separate the two properties explicitly. (a) For confidential drill-down, do NOT rely on watermark-freshness alone; require a SYNCHRONOUS authoritative revocation check (or a linearizable read of the authority's tombstone-version for the specific grant_id/record/subject) on the hot path for confidential tier, accepting the latency, OR (b) state the guarantee honestly as 'eventual revocation with a bounded propagation window during which already-authorized confidential access continues to be served — this is an ALLOW window, not a deny window' and make the leak-window a contractual exposure bound, not a correctness claim. The current text lets a reader believe revocation is effectively immediate-and-fail-closed; it is bounded-delay-and-fail-open-within-the-bound. Pick the synchronous check for the highest-sensitivity tier (export/ITAR-adjacent) and document the async window for the rest. Also add a 'revocation acknowledgment' protocol: the authority does not consider a revoke fully effective until all subscribed cells/shards ACK the tombstone version, and high-sensitivity grants can be configured to block reads until ACK.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: The central discovery index is a cross-tenant aggregation of per-tenant LSH codes whose non-invertibility is ASSERTED, not established, and the plan's own composition threat undermines it. LSH codes are designed to preserve neighborhood structure — that is precisely what makes them useful for candidate generation AND what makes them leak. Per-tenant salting/keying defends against cross-tenant linkage of identical inputs, but does NOT prevent (i) within-tenant membership inference (does institution B have a paper near query vector q?), (ii) reconstruction-by-triangulation when an adversary controls or colludes with one tenant and can issue crafted queries to map another tenant's code space, or (iii) the plan's own named composition channel: PSI overlap signal + index membership signal. The index ALSO carries discoverability scope, authority_version, and lease metadata per record — so even if the code itself were perfectly opaque, the EXISTENCE, COUNT, TOPICAL CLUSTER, and CHURN (publish/tombstone events) of a tenant's confidential-adjacent projections are observable to anyone with index query access and to the control-plane operator. 'Federation-scoped projection content minimized' is hand-waving; the metadata IS the leak.
why: The plan correctly identifies this as the existential surface (Risk #1) and correctly removes raw embeddings — but then re-creates a softer version of the same honeypot and declares it safe by fiat ('LSH codes only,' 'non-invertible,' 'per-tenant-keyed salted'). A reviewer at a target CISO's office will ask for the proof that these codes resist a vec2text-class or membership-inference attack under the platform's OWN adversary model (a colluding tenant + control-plane visibility), and the plan offers a Phase-2 red-team (Open Q #8) AFTER the index ships in Phase 2. That means the honeypot goes live before the privacy property is validated. For a confidentiality-first platform whose entire moat is 'confidential never centralizes,' shipping an unvalidated cross-tenant statistical honeypot is a contradiction of the core promise.
fix: Move the LSH/membership-inference red-team to a GATE that blocks central-index GA, not a Phase-2 open question. Specifically: (1) Default confidential-adjacent tenants to brokered-only (no index presence at all) — the plan says opt-out is the default for 'export-controlled/high-sensitivity' but should extend this to ALL confidential-derived projections; the index should hold PUBLIC-tier and explicitly-shared-federation-tier projections only, never anything derived from confidential corpora even after projection. (2) Treat index PRESENCE/COUNT/CHURN metadata as classified — add per-tenant differential-privacy noise or k-anonymity floors to count/existence queries, and encrypt scope+version metadata under per-consortium keys (the plan does this only for named-scope content, not for the existence signal). (3) Specify the concrete LSH scheme + a quantified bound (e.g. (ε,δ) membership-inference resistance) in shared/contracts, and make the red-team a release gate. Until then, do not claim the index is privacy-safe; claim only that it holds non-confidential-derived projections.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: Subject deprovisioning is only enforced for CONFIDENTIAL drill-down, leaving private/own-materials and any non-drill-down surface exposed for the full token TTL. Section 7.7 / 11.5 say 'Confidential drill-down requires a fresh subject-not-deprovisioned watermark within the leak-window' and 'very short confidential-tier token TTLs.' But a deprovisioned insider (terminated employee, revoked collaborator) retains: (a) access to PRIVATE(institution-internal) and private(self) tiers until their token expires — no subject-freshness check is specified there; (b) any cached/streamed session in progress; (c) discovery-index query access (the discovery PEP checks 'authz + discoverability scope' but the plan does not state it re-checks subject-not-deprovisioned freshness on every discovery query). The plan scopes the strongest control to the narrowest surface and leaves the broad surface on bearer-token-expiry semantics.
why: Insider deprovisioning is one of the most common real breach vectors in university environments (grad students leaving, visiting researchers, terminated staff with grievances). The threat model entry (SEC-med-deprovision) is treated as a confidential-tier-only problem, but the bulk of sensitive material a deprovisioned insider would exfiltrate is institution-private and own-materials — exactly the tiers with no subject-freshness gate. A breach here is a FERPA/contractual-confidentiality incident even if no cross-institution confidential record is touched.
fix: Extend the subject-not-deprovisioned freshness check to ALL non-public tiers and to the discovery PEP, not just confidential drill-down. Make subject-revocation a first-class check in the standard PEP decision flow (7.4) alongside grant/record freshness, evaluated on every request that touches private or confidential data. Keep token TTLs short across the board for non-public access, and add server-side session-kill that is triggered by the SCIM deprovision event (push, not pull) for in-flight sessions. State the residual exposure window explicitly per tier.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: Audit-before-serve and counter-signed reconciliation have no defined behavior when the two sides DISAGREE — the plan detects divergence but never says what divergence MEANS or triggers. Section 11.2 says 'Cells exchange counter-signed audit receipts; a two-sided reconciliation job detects divergence.' Detection without a defined response is not a control. What happens when grantor's log shows an access the grantee's log denies (or vice versa)? Is the grant frozen? Is it a P1? Is access suspended pending investigation? Is there a non-repudiation tie-break (whose signed record wins)? Additionally, 'an access that cannot be durably logged on the OWNER side is DENIED' protects the owner but says nothing about the requester-side record — a malicious owner could serve data and then claim it never logged the access (repudiation of disclosure), or log a DIFFERENT scope than it served. The receipts are counter-signed but the protocol for what the requester must counter-sign BEFORE receiving data (vs. after) is unspecified, which is the difference between a real non-repudiation protocol and two independent logs that happen to be signed.
why: For cross-institution confidential sharing, the audit log IS the legal artifact in a DUA/IP dispute. 'We detect divergence' with no defined consequence means the divergence is discovered post-hoc and resolved by lawyers, not by the system. The plan explicitly criticized v2 for 'a divergent/unbindable two-sided log is worthless as a legal artifact' and then ships a log whose divergence handling is undefined — re-creating the same flaw at a deeper level.
fix: Specify the protocol: (1) Requester must obtain a signed access-receipt FROM the owner that binds (grant_id, grant_version, scope, token_jti, watermark, timestamp) and the owner must obtain a signed request-receipt from the requester BEFORE bytes are served (fair-exchange / commit then serve). (2) Define divergence consequences: any reconciliation mismatch on a confidential grant auto-suspends that grant pending manual adjudication and raises a P1, with both signed records preserved. (3) Define the tie-break/non-repudiation rule (e.g., the owner-side WORM-anchored record is authoritative for disclosure; the requester-side record is authoritative for receipt). (4) Anchor both sides to the same external timestamp authority so 'who logged what when' is provable.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: Derived-data classification (MAX-tier join) is enforced at derivative CREATION but the embedding/LSH publication path for derivatives of confidential data is not blocked by the same rule — and the egress PEP re-derives classification of the SOURCE record, not of the projection's provenance. The plan says every derivative inherits MAX(input tiers) and that the egress PEP 're-derives/sanity-checks the source record's classification.' But an LSH code or expertise-fingerprint vector derived from a confidential corpus is itself a derivative whose tier should be confidential by the MAX rule — yet the index is explicitly designed to RECEIVE such codes ('per-tenant-keyed salted LSH codes for candidate generation' from confidential records, with brokered final scoring). So the system intentionally publishes a confidential-derived artifact to the shared plane. The egress PEP re-deriving the SOURCE classification would flag this as confidential and block it — UNLESS the projection is treated as a special-cased exception that bypasses the MAX rule. That exception is the leak path, and it is not reconciled: either the MAX rule holds (and LSH-from-confidential cannot be published, contradicting the index design) or it has a carve-out (which is exactly the kind of silent downgrade the dual-classifier was supposed to prevent).
why: This is an internal contradiction between two load-bearing controls (the MAX-tier derived-data rule + egress-PEP source-class re-derivation) and the central-index design. If a reviewer follows the type system honestly, publishing an LSH code derived from a confidential document is a tier violation; the plan resolves this implicitly by declaring the projection 'publishable,' which means the publishability decision OVERRIDES the classification — re-introducing trust in the projection-author's judgment that the dual-classifier and MAX-rule were meant to remove.
fix: Make the provenance explicit in the type system: a PublishableProjection carries the tier(s) of its source(s), and the egress PEP enforces that NO field (including LSH codes) derived from a confidential source may be published unless there is an explicit, audited, dual-classifier-agreed, grantor-consented declassification of THAT derived artifact — not just a per-field allowlist. Better: forbid confidential-derived codes in the index entirely (per finding #2's recommendation) and require brokered candidate generation for confidential corpora, so the MAX rule and the index design stop contradicting each other.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: Managed-cell operator is in the TCB AND holds the key-unwrap path AND runs the egress PEP — so for the default (Phase 0–1) deployment, the entire confidentiality guarantee reduces to trusting the vendor, yet the same deployment is sold to 'security-sensitive funded R1 centers' as the confidential-tier product. The plan is commendably honest that managed = bounded processor, not zero-trust, and reserves 'provably' for sovereign+TEE. But it does not reconcile that the Phase-0 beachhead (the FIRST revenue, the security-line-item buyer) is sold the MANAGED cell where the vendor can, by construction, read plaintext (key-unwrap held, no TEE, vendor runs the inference host and the egress gate). A 'security-sensitive' center's CISO doing real diligence will find that the vendor's own engineers and a compromised vendor control plane can read confidential research IP. The plan's mitigations (disabled swap, disabled core dumps, ephemeral DEK caching) are insider-deterrents, not insider-PREVENTION, and do nothing against a malicious-vendor or vendor-compromise scenario for the exact buyer who is paying for confidentiality.
why: The go-to-market sequence sells the WEAKEST trust tier to the buyer most likely to scrutinize trust. There is a real risk of a failed security review killing the beachhead deal precisely because the confidential-tier guarantee at N=1 is 'trust us' while the marketing says 'confidential never leaves your boundary.' This is both a security gap (vendor in TCB for confidential data) and a sales-integrity / sustainability gap.
fix: Either (a) bring TEE-backed inference into Phase-0 for the confidential tier of the managed beachhead cell (the plan already brings TEE early for export-class buyers — extend it to the security-sensitive R1 center, since that buyer's whole value prop is confidentiality), accepting the COGS hit; or (b) offer the beachhead buyer HYOK from day one (university holds the KEK, vendor cannot unwrap without university-held key) even on managed infra, so vendor-compromise cannot read plaintext-at-rest; or (c) be explicit in the sales motion and contract that the Phase-0 managed confidential tier is 'vendor-as-bounded-processor with contractual + technical controls, NOT cryptographically vendor-blind' and confirm in the Week-1 buyer-discovery spike that the beachhead CISO accepts that trust model. Do not let the gap between the marketed invariant and the managed-cell reality surface for the first time inside a paid security review.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: The per-shard revocation bitmap creates BOTH a fail-open staleness window per shard AND a covert membership/inference channel via the 'incomplete' security state. (a) Each shard refreshes its bitmap 'within the leak-window'; a revocation committed to the authority is not in a given shard's bitmap until that shard refreshes — so the same fail-open-within-the-window problem as finding #1 applies per shard, and a shard reporting itself fresh can still surface a just-revoked hit. (b) The 'incomplete' flag is returned to the QUERYING user. Because incomplete fires when a shard's bitmap is stale, and bitmap staleness correlates with revocation churn on specific topical clusters (shards are sharded BY TOPIC), an adversary issuing topical queries can use the incomplete signal as a side channel to infer WHICH topical areas are experiencing revocation activity across the federation — i.e., which research areas have confidential grants being pulled. Topical-cluster sharding + per-shard completeness leakage = a cross-institution research-activity inference channel.
why: The plan elevates 'incomplete' from a quality flag to a security state (good) but does not consider that exposing a security state to the querier is itself an information disclosure. Combined with topic-based sharding, the completeness signal leaks the rhythm of confidential collaboration changes across institutions — exactly the kind of competitive/strategic intelligence the confidentiality tier exists to protect.
fix: (1) Apply finding #1's synchronous-check or ACK-protocol logic to the bitmap: a shard should not surface a hit whose tombstone-version it cannot prove it has seen; consider serving the candidate but gating final reveal through the owning cell's synchronous check (the plan already brokers final scoring — push the authoritative revocation check there too, making the index bitmap a performance optimization, not the authority). (2) Do NOT expose raw 'incomplete' per-shard to the user; aggregate completeness coarsely, add noise, or fail the WHOLE query closed rather than leaking per-shard/per-topic completeness. (3) Decouple shard topology from topic for the completeness signal, or add cover traffic, so incomplete cannot be correlated to a topical cluster's revocation activity.
