lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality — end-to-end enforcement of public/private/confidential, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR, FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness
issue: BYO-endpoint for confidential routing trusts an unverified institutional ATTESTATION that the endpoint is in-boundary — an attestation-spoofing / misconfiguration leak path straight through the router's strongest invariant. Section 8.2: 'BYO may point at a self-hosted endpoint for confidential ONLY if the institution attests it is in-boundary.' This is a self-asserted claim by the tenant admin, with no technical verification that the attested endpoint is actually (a) in the data-residency region, (b) not a proxy to a cloud frontier model, (c) not logging/retaining prompts, (d) actually the model it claims. The entire 'confidential routes local only' invariant — which the plan pins, tests, and hard-fails on — has a configuration-time bypass: an admin (compromised, mistaken, or malicious) attests a cloud-proxying endpoint as in-boundary, and confidential prompts flow to a frontier provider while the system believes it is compliant. The transport-policy hard-fail checks the router's DECISION against intrinsic tier, but if the configured 'local' endpoint is secretly remote, transport policy sees a 'local' selection and passes it.
why: This is a direct end-run around the confidential-never-leaves-boundary guarantee, gated only by a checkbox attestation. For a platform whose moat is structural confidentiality, allowing confidential inference to a BYO endpoint validated by trust-me is a contradiction. It is also a deemed-export and GDPR-transfer hole: confidential ITAR-adjacent or EU-personal-data prompts could egress to a US/foreign cloud through a mis-attested 'in-boundary' endpoint.
fix: For confidential-tier BYO endpoints, require TECHNICAL verification, not attestation: (1) network-level proof the endpoint resolves and routes within the tenant's declared boundary/region (egress-policy-enforced, not attested); (2) endpoint attestation via mTLS + a challenge that confirms the model identity and a no-retention contract enforced where possible (TEE attestation of the BYO host for the strongest tier); (3) for export-controlled/EU-personal data, FORBID BYO confidential endpoints unless they pass the sovereign-tier verification. Treat the in-boundary claim as an assertion that must be technically validated before any confidential prompt is routed there, and audit every confidential inference with the verified endpoint identity.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, contract clarity, coupling, testability, monorepo-vs-services, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The TierLattice 'compiler-enforced exhaustiveness check' that gates a new tier across all five consumers (router, MAX-derivation, egress allowlist, Cedar narrow-only, edition-posture derivation) is the load-bearing safety mechanism for the most cross-cutting concept in the system — but it cannot exist as described. The consumers are independently-deployed services (modules are 'deployed as independent service/Helm sub-chart', cells run different substrate profiles, nodes upgrade independently). There is no shared compilation unit across a managed cell, a sovereign single-appliance, and the central exchange, so a single typed total-match check cannot 'FAIL THE BUILD' across all of them. Python's type system has no exhaustive enum match that fails compilation regardless (it requires mypy + assert_never wiring per call site, and only catches sites that actually pattern-match — a consumer that does dict.get(tier) or a runtime config lookup is invisible to it).
why: The plan's own decision log says 'adding a tier is a checklisted, test-gated policy-kernel release' and that the exhaustiveness check prevents a tier change from silently shipping a confidentiality leak (Risk #25). If the check is partial (catches some consumers, misses runtime-dispatch and remote consumers), then the team will believe a new tier is fully handled when it is not — the most dangerous failure mode, because it removes the human vigilance the checklist was supposed to enforce. A new tier that the egress allowlist or a remote node's projection logic doesn't handle is a leak, and this is sold as the thing that prevents exactly that.
fix: Stop claiming compiler-enforced exhaustiveness across distributed consumers. Make the tier set a versioned artifact in shared/contracts/ with an explicit per-consumer conformance registry: each consumer (including each deployed node/substrate) must publish, at startup and in CI, a signed attestation that it handles tier set version N, and the control plane MUST refuse to activate tier N+1 until every live consumer has attested support. Pair mypy assert_never (which genuinely works for in-process pattern matches) with an explicit runtime registry test that enumerates every consumer's declared tier coverage. Treat unhandled-tier as a fail-closed runtime deny, not just a build gate, because the build gate provably cannot cover remote independently-versioned consumers.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, contract clarity, coupling, testability, monorepo-vs-services, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The kernel is a god-object by accumulation and the plan never bounds it. It owns: TierLattice, IClassifier, IPolicyEnforcement, IAuditSink, IPublishMetadata, IModelRouter, IRetrievalPlanner+orchestrator, IIdentityResolution, IEventBus, IExpertiseFingerprint, ICollaborationGraph, plus the cross-kernel descriptor/AuditEvent/OutboxRecord contracts. Every one of the four feature modules depends on the kernel; the kernel depends on nothing. The 'two coherence domains' (policy vs serving) split is asserted but the cross-kernel contract list (descriptor + AuditEvent + OutboxRecord) shows serving and policy are tightly coupled at runtime (serving emits audit that policy consumes; serving produces outbox that policy's egress PEP validates). There is no kernel-wide version, no statement of expected kernel change frequency, and no mechanism to add a kernel capability without every module potentially needing a contract-version bump.
why: Modularity is measured by blast radius. When the single component everything depends on grows monotonically and is never decomposed or version-bounded, every kernel change is a potential fan-out to all four modules and both substrate profiles. This is the classic 'shared kernel rots into the bottleneck' failure the plan claims to avoid for IExpertiseGraph but reintroduces at a larger scale for the kernel itself. The decision log explicitly says it split IExpertiseGraph because 'a contract one module writes and all four read is the classic bottleneck where v1 rotted' — yet the kernel is precisely that pattern with eleven contracts instead of one.
fix: Bound the kernel explicitly: (1) define what is NOT allowed in the kernel (no feature logic, no module-specific schemas), (2) version the policy kernel and serving kernel independently with stated compatibility windows and an expected-change-frequency budget per contract (e.g., TierLattice is frozen-after-Phase-1, IModelRouter expected to churn), (3) make IIdentityResolution and the read-model projections candidates for extraction into their own versioned services rather than kernel-resident, since they are not security-critical and their churn shouldn't force kernel releases. State the rule: a contract whose change frequency differs by an order of magnitude from the rest of its kernel must be evicted from that kernel.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, contract clarity, coupling, testability, monorepo-vs-services, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: 'Additive-only forever' is the declared evolution rule for PublishableProjection, IExpertiseFingerprint, ICollaborationGraph, AuditEvent, OutboxRecord, and ClassificationCapabilityDescriptor — but additive-only with no exercised removal path is an unmaintainability guarantee, not a maintainability feature. The plan gestures at a 'per-field consumer registry' and 'a field is retired when no pillar reads it' and 'removal-time dependency check' (Section 5.7), but the central-index field-intersection mechanism means a field added in node version N must be supported by the index essentially forever to keep older nodes working, and there is no stated process for ever shrinking the projection. Over years of independently-upgrading nodes, the publishable surface and the read-models monotonically grow.
why: The PublishableProjection is the security-critical federation seam — every field on it is an allowlisted leak surface that must carry a classification-review record. An ever-growing, never-shrinking allowlist means the audit surface and the field-intersection logic across deployed node versions grows without bound, the cross-version contract tests (node@N vs index@M) multiply combinatorially, and the 'classification review per field' debt accumulates. A seam that can only grow is a seam whose blast radius and review cost grow with age — the definition of architectural rot at the highest-stakes boundary.
fix: Specify a real deprecation lifecycle, not just additive growth: per-field introduced-in-version AND deprecated-in-version AND removed-in-version, with a defined minimum-node-version floor (the index drops support for projection fields older than the oldest supported node version, and forces stragglers to upgrade on a stated cadence). Make the consumer-usage registry authoritative: a field with zero consumers for K releases is auto-flagged for removal with a migration. Cap the cross-version test matrix by maintaining at most M supported projection versions, not all historical versions.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, contract clarity, coupling, testability, monorepo-vs-services, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The monorepo-vs-services boundary is internally contradictory and produces a distributed monolith. Section 5.2/5.7 say modules are 'deployed as independent service/Helm sub-chart' and never import each other. But Section 5.11/6.3 say all four modules share ONE consolidated Postgres in the MVP, isolated only by per-module schemas + DB-role grants, and the split into separate stores is deferred behind a trigger (2M docs / 5M nodes / 60% RAM). Independently-deployed services sharing a single physical database with cross-schema grants is the textbook distributed monolith: you pay the operational cost of services (network calls, partial-failure, deploy coordination) while retaining the coupling cost of a shared store (shared failure domain, shared connection pool, shared backup/restore, coordinated migrations, contention — which the plan itself flags in 6.3). The DB-role-grant isolation is real for read-access but does nothing for the shared-failure-domain and shared-resource-contention coupling.
why: This undermines the central modularity claim ('add/remove a feature with minimal blast radius'). A module deployed as a service but backed by the shared Postgres cannot be independently scaled, independently failed-over, or independently migrated without coordinating with every other module's schema in the same instance. The split trigger means the architecture's coupling profile silently changes under load precisely when the smallest, cheapest cells have the least headroom — and the plan admits contention bites there. You get neither clean service isolation nor clean monolith simplicity.
fix: Pick the honest posture for the MVP: a modular monolith (single deployable, in-process module boundaries enforced by import-linter + DB schemas) is the correct choice for a 3-engineer team and one edition — say so, and drop the 'deployed as independent service' language until a module actually needs independent scaling. Reserve service extraction as a designed seam (the plan already has the design-vs-build discipline elsewhere). If modules truly must be separate services from day one, then they cannot share a Postgres instance — give each module its own database and make cross-module data flow exclusively via the event bus/read-models, which is what the plan already claims is the only allowed path.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, contract clarity, coupling, testability, monorepo-vs-services, swappability of AI-router and retrieval
issue: The router/transport 'disagreement = hard fail' contract proves the router is NOT actually swappable in the way claimed — it makes correctness depend on a second component (ITransportPolicy) independently re-deriving the same tier-routing logic. The plan frames the hard-fail as the safety net that lets the router be replaceable, but a buggy or malicious replacement router that selects cloud for confidential will simply hard-fail every confidential inference, taking the product down rather than leaking. That is safe but it means 'swap the router' is not a low-blast-radius operation: any router that disagrees with transport on tier semantics is a hard outage, so the router and transport are semantically coupled through the tier lattice they must both interpret identically.
why: The locked requirement is a provider-agnostic, swappable router. The plan delivers a router that is swappable for provider-selection-among-cloud-options but is rigidly bound to ITransportPolicy on tier-to-locality mapping. If the routing-precedence invariant ('confidential ALWAYS local') ever needs to change, it must change in lockstep in two places (router selection logic AND transport validation), which is exactly the cross-cutting fan-out the plan elsewhere tries to eliminate. A reviewer should not accept 'two components independently implement the same rule' as decoupling — it is duplicated logic that must be kept in sync, the inverse of a single source of truth.
fix: Make the tier-to-locality mapping a single owned policy in the TierLattice/routing-policy table that BOTH the router consults and the transport enforces — not two independent re-derivations. The router asks the policy 'what localities are legal for this tier?' and selects within that set; the transport enforces the same policy as a final gate. Then the router truly only owns provider-preference within an already-constrained legal set, the disagreement case becomes a router that violated the policy it was given (a clear bug class), and changing the precedence invariant is a single-table edit covered by the exhaustiveness mechanism rather than a two-site synchronized change.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The Phase-0 MVP is no longer the locked product — it is a single-tenant confidential RAG tool for ONE lab, and the entire differentiated value (the federation seam) is deferred to Phase 1, yet the competitive analysis (1.2) and white-space claim are built ENTIRELY on confidential federation. At N=1 there is no federation, no cross-institution sharing, no exchange layer. So the Phase-0 product the first dollar pays for sits squarely in a market the plan itself says is commoditized/free: 'AI-grounded Q&A + semantic search + expert discovery over the center's OWN corpus.' That is exactly what NotebookLM Enterprise, Glean, ChatGPT Enterprise w/ connectors, Elicit/Scholar workspaces, and every vertical RAG vendor already sell — most with bigger teams and SOC2 already done. The plan's stated moat ('Nobody federates confidential cross-institution research data') does not apply to the thing being sold first.
why: First revenue is being earned on a product whose differentiation the plan never establishes. The 'differentiated security guarantee at N=1' (local-only inference + egress block + per-tenant keys) is not unique — on-prem/VPC-isolated RAG with no-data-leaves-your-boundary is table stakes for every enterprise RAG vendor selling to regulated buyers. A funded R1 center evaluating a 3-person startup's single-cell RAG tool against incumbents with the same security posture and a real support org has no reason to choose the startup. The plan validates buyer DESIRE (Week-1 spike) but never validates that the Phase-0 product WINS against incumbents on its own merits without the federation moat.
fix: Add an explicit competitive teardown of the Phase-0 single-cell product specifically — name NotebookLM Enterprise, Glean, ChatGPT Enterprise, and any research-vertical RAG incumbent, and state in one sentence why a funded center picks you at N=1 BEFORE the network exists. If the honest answer is 'research-native graph + SPECTER2 expertise fingerprints + scholarly-corpus ingestion,' say that explicitly and make it the Phase-0 wedge thesis. If the honest answer is 'they don't, the federation roadmap is the reason they buy early,' then the dyad cannot be truly decoupled and the runway model must absorb a longer pre-revenue period. Do not let the Phase-0 product inherit the federation moat by association.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The single most expensive validation — value-based WTP at or above the COGS floor — is scheduled as a pre-build interview (2.6, 19.3), but the BYO-compute escape hatch makes the whole pricing thesis circular and untested. The plan says: validate WTP; if WTP < COGS floor, make BYO-compute the default. But BYO-compute shifts GPU COGS to the buyer, which means the buyer now must procure, provision, and operate a GPU node for a 3-person vendor's software — a far heavier IT lift that triggers MORE procurement friction (the very veto graph in 2.8), not less. The plan treats BYO-compute as a clean economic escape when it is actually an adoption-friction escalation. And the $2.5-8k/mo ASP band is asserted, not derived from any comparable; the only named comparable (Atom at ~$179/mo) is 14-45x cheaper.
why: A 14-45x price gap to the nearest named comparable in the same buyer's budget, justified only by 'confidentiality + a security line-item exists,' is the kind of assumption that sinks the unit economics if wrong. If WTP lands at, say, $1-2k/mo (closer to enterprise SaaS seat-bundles than to a clean-room contract), the BYO-compute fallback doesn't save the model — it makes the product harder to sell AND shifts you into competing with free/cheap RAG on software-only pricing. The plan never models the BYO-compute ASP or its adoption-friction cost.
fix: Before build, get a written price indication (not just 'desire') from at least 2 of the 3 candidate centers anchored to a concrete budget line they would draw from (clean-room/secure-collaboration budget vs research-software budget — these are 10x apart). Model the BYO-compute scenario explicitly: software-only ASP, the buyer-side ops burden, and which veto offices that ops burden re-triggers. State the floor ASP below which the business is not viable and treat the Week-1 spike as a kill-gate, not a calibration.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The 'one product serves all four buyers' claim is satisfied architecturally (editions + packaging) but the GTM is NOT credibly sequenced. The plan admits a 'one-GTM-hire team runs one motion at a time' (2.2), then assigns: Phase-0 = single-center direct BD, Phase-1 = consortium multi-party BD, Phase-2 = top-down institutional field sales + PLG loss-leader, Phase-3 = executive selling + grant monetization. That is FOUR distinct enterprise motions, each requiring different collateral, sales cycles, champions, and procurement navigation, run sequentially by one GTM person across ~3 years. Consortium BD (multi-party, multi-legal-entity) and top-down institutional field sales are different jobs requiring different hires; the plan defers the hire but not the motion.
why: The locked requirement is one PRODUCT for four buyers, which the plan meets. But commercial viability depends on actually executing the motions, and a single non-specialist GTM hire cannot run consortium multi-party BD (the hardest enterprise motion that exists — you're herding 2+ universities' legal/IT/research offices simultaneously) in Phase 1 while also having closed a center in Phase 0 and prepping institutional sales for Phase 2. The roadmap implicitly assumes serial single-threaded GTM execution with no specialist hires until Phase 2, which is not how multi-party institutional deals actually close.
fix: State the GTM hiring plan against the motion sequence: which motion needs which specialist hire, and when. If consortium BD is Phase-1, a consortium/alliance BD specialist (or a founder with existing consortium relationships) must be in place BEFORE Phase 1, not deferred to Phase 2. Tie the runway model (16.2) to GTM headcount, not just engineering. Acknowledge that 'one motion at a time' with one hire likely stretches each phase's GTM ramp by quarters beyond the engineering timeline.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The race-window thesis (1.5, Risk #17) — 'lock ≥2 consortia under multi-year contracts before a funded incumbent ships confidential federation' — is the entire defensibility argument, and the timeline math defeats it. By the plan's own roadmap, the first dyad contract is Phase-1 (Q4-Q5), the first institutional SaaS is month 15-18, and '≥2 consortia under multi-year contracts' is a PHASE-3 milestone (Section 18). Phase 3 is realistically year 2.5-3+. The plan asks a 3-person team to win the race by year 3 against an incumbent (Atom, 50+ logos, funded) who, per the plan's own admission, 'can add per-tenant isolation as an engineering project faster than we can acquire their distribution.' The defensibility milestone lands AFTER the window the plan says is the threat.
why: If the moat is a race and the race is won at Phase 3 (~year 3) while a funded incumbent with distribution can ship isolation in an engineering quarter or two, the plan's own logic says you lose the race. The compounding assets named (sharing-grant graph, trust relationships, certs) only accumulate AFTER you have multiple federated nodes — which is Phase 2-3. There is a multi-year window where you have the differentiation built but not the contractual lock, and that is precisely when an incumbent's distribution beats your moat.
fix: Either (a) compress the path to multi-year consortium lock dramatically (sign LOIs/multi-year framework deals with consortia in Phase 0-1 BEFORE the full federation product ships, using the roadmap as the lock mechanism), or (b) honestly down-grade the defensibility claim from 'we win the race' to 'we occupy a niche the incumbent won't prioritize' and identify which sub-segment (export-adjacent? specific consortia like CTSA?) is too small/regulated for the incumbent to chase. The current framing claims a race you're sequenced to lose.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The beachhead buyer is internally contradictory on the dimension that justifies the price. The Phase-0 buyer is defined as a 'single funded R1 center / security-sensitive lab (NOT export-controlled)' that has 'a security line-item budget.' But the plan also says (3.3, 16.1) the differentiator is confidential-tier local-only inference. A center that is genuinely security-sensitive ENOUGH to pay $2.5-8k/mo for no-data-leaves-the-boundary is very often export-adjacent, defense-funded, or handling regulated data (the exact buyers the plan pushes to the deferred Phase-2+ sovereign/CMMC vertical). Strip out export-controlled, defense, and CMMC-requiring buyers, and the remaining set — a non-regulated R1 center that nonetheless pays a premium for in-boundary inference on its OWN internal corpus — is a much narrower and less-proven segment than the plan implies.
why: The plan resolves the export-lab over-claim correctly (export buyers need sovereign/CMMC, deferred). But in doing so it carves out the buyers with the strongest, most-budgeted reason to pay for confidentiality, and leaves a Phase-0 segment whose willingness to pay a premium for confidentiality-on-internal-data is asserted, not evidenced. A non-export R1 center can often use the university's existing NotebookLM Enterprise / Azure OpenAI VPC tenancy for 'data stays in our boundary' at far lower marginal cost.
fix: Name the specific non-export segment that has both the budget AND a confidentiality requirement that incumbent VPC-tenancy RAG does NOT satisfy. Candidates: industry-sponsored research labs under corporate NDA/IP-confidentiality (not export, but contractually confidential), translational/clinical centers with PHI under BAA, or labs with pre-publication IP-sensitivity. Pick one, validate it specifically in Week 1, and make it the named beachhead — 'security-sensitive R1 center' is too vague to be a beachhead.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The procurement/adoption-friction analysis (2.8, Risk #19) honestly maps a 4-6 office veto graph but then under-budgets its TIME impact against the runway model. The plan ships pre-empting collateral (HECVAT pre-fill, OGC DPA package, IRB data-flow) — good — but the revenue-by-quarter model (16.2) shows 'first pilot live' in Q2 (month ~6). A funded R1 center's security review + OGC sign-off + (if any human-subjects or licensed-corpus data is touched) IRB/library review for a NEW SaaS vendor with no SOC2 yet routinely takes 3-9 months on its own. The plan's own SOC2 timing (Type I at Phase-0→1 boundary, Type II at month 15-18) means the Phase-0 pilot is being sold to a security-conscious buyer BEFORE either SOC2 exists.
why: Selling a confidential-data product to a security-line-item buyer with NO SOC2 (not even Type I) is the single biggest procurement blocker for exactly this buyer type — CISOs gate on it. The plan acknowledges 'design partners start on a questionnaire + roadmap,' but a questionnaire-and-roadmap rarely clears a security review for production confidential data at an R1. The Q2 first-pilot date is likely optimistic by 1-3 quarters, which compounds the runway problem (16.2 plans 24-30 months; a 2-quarter slip on first revenue + the SOC2-gated recurring revenue is material).
fix: Reconcile the SOC2 timeline with the buyer's security gate explicitly. Either (a) the Phase-0 pilot is structured as a NON-production / de-identified / sandbox pilot that doesn't trip the full security review (state this — it changes what 'confidential tier' means in the pilot), or (b) pull SOC2 Type I fully into Phase 0 and re-cost it against the 3-engineer team (the plan currently defers it to the Phase-0→1 boundary). Stress-test the runway model with first-pilot slipping to Q3-Q4 and first-recurring to month 18-24.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: Phase-0 'single-cell, 3-engineer-buildable' scope is materially larger than the plan admits. The Section 18 Phase-0 build list includes, simultaneously: a dual independent classifier with agreement logic, a pure router + independent ITransportPolicy enforcement layer + router-disagreement hard-fail, IPolicyEnforcement/PEP-PDP abstraction over library RBAC, hash-chained IAuditSink, deterministic identity resolution (anchors+blocking+graph-feature classifier), the IRetrievalPlanner+orchestrator+micro-interface retrieval seam, a functional confidential tier (vLLM + per-tenant Vault/KMS keys + network egress block), a consolidated Postgres with pgvector+AGE+tsvector+audit+outbox AND per-module schemas with DB-role grants, a Dagster pipeline, the owned TierLattice module + typed exhaustiveness check + import-linter + DB-permission CI gates, a scaffolded local docker-compose 2-cell harness, an AGE/PPR synthetic benchmark, RAGAS-in-CI with a GPU-staging judge, and the versioned PublishableProjection contract types. Much of this is platform/abstraction work (the kernel, the contract machinery, the CI gates, the modular DB isolation) that delivers ZERO customer-visible value but consumes the bulk of senior-engineer time. A pre-revenue 3-person team building all of this before first dollar is the classic 'we built the platform before we found out if anyone wanted the product' failure.
why: This is the founder-feasibility crux. If Phase-0 realistically takes 9-12 months for 3 engineers rather than the ~2 quarters the burn model implies, the pre-recurring-revenue valley (already 12-18mo to SaaS) blows past the stated 24-30mo runway. The plan's own runway math depends on Phase-0 shipping a paid pilot by Q2.
fix: Ruthlessly separate 'walking-skeleton MVP that proves the wedge' from 'platform investment.' For Phase-0, defer the kernel/contract/exhaustiveness/DB-role/local-harness machinery to Phase-1 and ship the single-cell confidential RAG product as a deliberately-monolithic, less-modular app (the modularity tax is only justified once a second module/edition/cell exists). Re-derive the Phase-0 timeline bottom-up from the actual build list and reconcile it against the Q2-first-pilot assumption in Section 16.2.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: The burn/runway model (Section 16.2) omits the single largest cost line for a startup: loaded engineering + GTM salaries. It models infra COGS ($1.5-3k/cell), SOC2 (~$40-70k/yr), and staging, but '5 FTE' loaded cost (roughly $150-250k/yr each fully loaded = ~$0.9-1.25M/yr) never appears in the runway arithmetic. The '24-30 months runway' assertion is therefore unanchored to a monthly burn number.
why: Runway = cash / monthly burn, and burn is ~90% salaries at this stage, not infra. A runway claim that excludes payroll is not a runway model. This directly affects how much seed must be raised and whether the 12-18mo-to-recurring valley is survivable, which is the exact question the section purports to answer.
fix: Add a real monthly-burn line: 5 FTE loaded + fractional GRC/counsel + infra + tooling + audit, summed to a monthly number, divided into the seed to produce the runway figure. Then show first-dollar (Q2 pilot) and first-material-recurring (mo 15-18) against that burn so the cash-out date is explicit. Without this, the seed-size ask is unjustified.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: The beachhead buyer is asserted, not evidenced, and the ENTIRE sequence is gated on a Week-1 spike finding it. The wedge is 'a single funded R1 center / security-sensitive lab with a security line-item budget that will pay $2.5-8k/mo for an internal AI-grounded Q&A/search tool over its OWN corpus, with no federation and no second institution.' That is a single-university RAG tool with a confidential tier, i.e., very close to what TigerResearchBuddy already is, minus polish. The differentiator at N=1 is 'the data never leaves the boundary,' but a lab can already run Ollama/vLLM + a vector DB on its own hardware for near-zero marginal cost. WTP of $30k-96k/yr for that, from a single lab, before any cross-institution value exists, is the load-bearing assumption of the whole plan and has no evidence behind it.
why: If the N=1 confidential-RAG value isn't worth $2.5-8k/mo to a single center (because they can self-host the open-source stack, or because internal-only RAG isn't a budgeted pain), there is no Phase-0 revenue, and the dyad (Phase-1) is even harder. The plan's federation moat only matters at N>=2; at N=1 you are selling a commodity the buyer can assemble from free parts.
fix: Make the Week-1 spike a hard go/no-go gate with a falsifiable threshold (e.g., 'at least 2 of 3 named centers give a written price indication >= $30k/yr for internal-only deployment'), and pre-commit to pivoting the wedge if it fails. Sharpen the N=1 value prop beyond 'data stays in-boundary' (which self-hosting also gives), e.g., the managed/compliance/audit burden you remove, or the library-corpus connector, and validate THAT is the willingness-to-pay driver, not federation.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: The 'designed-only vs built-now' split is blurry precisely where it costs the most, and several heavy items are placed in Phase-0. Section 2.5 says 'through Phase-1 the only fully-built topology is the single-cell managed Institution edition,' and PLG scheduler / sovereign image / BYO-compute are 'design-reserved seams with zero implementation.' But Phase-0 (Section 18) still builds the owned TierLattice with compiler-enforced exhaustiveness across all consumers, per-module DB-schema isolation with role grants and a migration check, the local 2-cell docker-compose harness (scaffolded), AGE PPR benchmarking, and the versioned PublishableProjection contract, all of which are modular-platform investments whose payoff only arrives at Phase-1+. The 'interfaces present, zero implementation' principle is not applied to these.
why: Designing five isolation postures and a contract/exhaustiveness regime up front is exactly the over-engineering the plan elsewhere warns against. A team selling ONE edition for 18 months pays a large abstraction tax for variation points it won't exercise until Phase 2+. This is the difference between a Phase-0 that ships in 4-5 months and one that ships in 10.
fix: Apply the 'interface present, impl deferred' rule consistently: in Phase-0 the TierLattice can be a simple enum + MAX function (no compiler-enforced cross-consumer exhaustiveness machinery until a second tier-consumer exists), DB isolation can be import-linter-only (DB-role grants when a second module ships), and the 2-cell harness can be deferred to the start of Phase-1 (you have no second cell to test until then). Move the AGE/PPR benchmark to the Phase-0/1 boundary unless the graph is actually on the Phase-0 critical path.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: Per-cell COGS floor is still optimistic in two ways that compress margin. (1) The GPU line ($735-1454/mo) is on-demand list for an A10G/L4 24GB instance running a 7-8B model; under real concurrent load with TEE (required early for the operator-zero-trust/export-class buyers the plan now courts in Phase-1) and any headroom you move to larger/more instances, and TEE-capable GPU instances carry a premium. (2) The central index is assigned to a managed service (OpenSearch Serverless / managed vector) to keep ops off 3 engineers, but managed serverless search has a non-trivial IDLE billing FLOOR (OpenSearch Serverless bills minimum OCUs, ~several hundred $/mo even at zero traffic), and that floor exists from the FIRST consortium node, not at scale, yet it's described as 'amortized via the exchange add-on' which only works once you have paying consortium customers.
why: A $1.5-3k/mo floor that is really $3-5k/mo once you add TEE-capable compute, warm vLLM, and the managed-index idle floor materially changes the breakeven customer count and the BYO-compute decision. The plan's whole unit-economics argument rests on this floor.
fix: Re-cost the GPU line specifically for TEE-capable instances (which the Phase-1 export/zero-trust buyer requires) and state that premium. Surface the managed-index IDLE billing floor as a fixed cost that exists from the first federated node and decide who eats it before consortium revenue arrives. Re-run the breakeven-customer-count with the corrected floor.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: Per-cell isolated Vault Community 'one Vault per cell' is presented as a cost win (no Enterprise license) but is silently an OPS cost. Each managed confidential cell now runs its own Vault Community instance that must be unsealed, backed up, key-rotated, monitored, and upgraded. At N cells that is N Vault instances for a 3-engineer team, plus the unseal/recovery operational burden Vault is notorious for. The plan counts the license savings ($0-100/mo) but not the per-cell operational toil.
why: The plan's stated principle is to keep ops off the 3 engineers (it does this for the central index by going managed). Running N self-operated Vault instances directly contradicts that for the most operationally finicky component in the stack; Vault unseal/recovery incidents are a classic small-team time sink.
fix: Default to cloud KMS per-tenant keys (the stated alternative) rather than per-cell Vault Community, since KMS is managed and eliminates the unseal/backup/upgrade toil entirely. Reserve self-run Vault for sovereign/on-prem only (where the university operates it). State the per-cell Vault operational cost explicitly if it is kept anywhere managed.

lens: Cost, ops & founder feasibility
issue: SpiceDB 'cell-local replica' is treated as a configuration choice but is custom infrastructure the plan must BUILD and OPERATE. SpiceDB has no native 'replicate a subset of tuples into a regional read-replica cell with home-minted ZedToken carry' primitive. The plan's revocation-correctness story, partition behavior, and the p99<50ms cell-local Check SLO all depend on this replication layer existing and being correct, yet it is listed as a Phase-1 build item alongside the revocation authority, the freshness watermark feed, the per-shard revocation bitmaps, and the egress PEP. That is a dense cluster of bespoke distributed-systems infrastructure for a 3-person team in a single phase.
why: Bespoke cross-region authz replication with ordering/freshness guarantees is genuinely hard distributed-systems work, the kind that takes a dedicated team quarters to get right and harden. Compressing it into Phase-1 alongside everything else is the schedule risk most likely to slip and the one most likely to ship a subtle correctness bug (which here is a confidentiality leak).
fix: Validate in the Week-1/Phase-0 spike (already Open Q #5) whether the cell-local replication can be done with SpiceDB's actual primitives or whether it requires building a replication layer, and if the latter, treat it as its own multi-month workstream with a dedicated owner, not a line item. Consider whether Phase-1 can ship with synchronous cross-region Checks (simpler, slower, available) and defer cell-local replication until latency actually demands it.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation + exchange + control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The synchronous authoritative revocation check sits on the un-sheddable confidential drill-down hot path AND is multi-region AND must fail-closed under authority partition AND must hold a 6.5s composite SLO. These four constraints are mutually corrosive and the plan resolves the tension by asserting a single unsourced number ('~80-150ms p99'). A synchronous check against a multi-region consensus authority (Risk #4: 'multi-region consensus') on the hot path inherits that consensus's tail latency and its CAP posture: under a control-plane partition you chose fail-closed (correct for confidentiality), which means every confidential drill-down DENIES during any authority partition or leader election. That is not 'metadata-only degradation' — it is a total outage of the flagship confidential-collaboration feature (locked Feature #3) precisely when the network is stressed. The plan never derives p99 from the consensus protocol (Raft/Paxos round trips + cross-region RTT + HSM signing latency), never bounds leader-election stall time against the SLO, and never reconciles 'synchronous consensus check per request' with 'horizontally scalable' (NFR) — a per-request synchronous global authority is an anti-scaling serialization point.
why: This is the load-bearing fix for the 'v3 leak' and the plan's headline claim ('the v3 leak is closed'). If the latency/availability math doesn't close, the choice is between (a) relaxing to async/allow-window on confidential data (reopening the leak the plan claims to have closed) or (b) accepting frequent confidential-feature outages under normal cloud partition rates. Either reopens a locked requirement (end-to-end enforced confidentiality, or horizontal scalability).
fix: Derive the budget, don't assert it. Specify the authority's consensus protocol and quorum geometry, then compute worst-case hot-path latency = client→cell RTT + cell→authority-quorum RTT (cross-region) + consensus commit + HSM sign, with a separate leader-election stall bound. Then either: (a) make the authority READ path lease-based and locally-replicated so the hot-path check is a local read of a fenced lease (not a cross-region consensus round trip) with a bounded lease TTL that defines the actual fail-open window — and state that TTL as the honest confidential allow-window; or (b) keep synchronous-cross-region only for a narrow 'highest-sensitivity' subset and publish the measured outage minutes/year that fail-closed-under-partition implies at realistic partition rates. Open Question #4 must report a measured number from the Phase-1 spike as a release gate, not a target.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation + exchange + control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: Cell-local SpiceDB 'replication + revision-translation table' for monotonic carried-ZedToken reads is named as a Phase-1 deliverable with a 'fallback to synchronous cross-region Checks,' but the plan itself flags (Risk #28, OQ #5) it may be a bespoke build. SpiceDB's consistency model is Zookie/ZedToken-based 'at-least-as-fresh' reads against its own datastore; there is no first-class primitive for replicating the relationship graph to a remote cell AND translating revisions so a carried ZedToken from the authority maps to a monotonic local revision. Building that correctly is a distributed-consistency project (causal-token translation across two independent revision spaces). The 'fallback to synchronous cross-region Checks' is not a smaller thing — it is finding #1's per-request cross-region latency applied to EVERY authorization check, not just revocation.
why: Authorization is on every private/confidential request and every discovery hit (Risk #14: 'on every private/confidential request'). If cell-local replication doesn't pan out, the fallback puts a cross-region Check on the entire read path, blowing the p95<800ms discovery SLO (Phase 2) and the 6.5s confidential SLO simultaneously. This is a single unproven primitive that two SLOs depend on, gated behind a Week-1 spike that may simply say 'no.'
fix: Treat this as a go/no-go architectural fork, not a fallback. Before Phase 1 build, prototype ZedToken→local-revision translation against SpiceDB's actual replication story (watch/LookupResources API + local materialized cache with a staleness bound) and MEASURE replication lag + fail-closed-on-lag behavior. If translation can't guarantee monotonic reads under lag, do not ship 'synchronous cross-region Check on every request' — cache authorization decisions cell-locally with a short bounded TTL and synchronous re-check ONLY on the confidential-sensitive subset (collapsing into finding #1's design). State the cache staleness bound as a first-class consistency guarantee.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation + exchange + control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: CDC sync mechanics (Debezium + Kafka, per-tenant partitioned, tombstone coalescing) are named but correctness-critical semantics are undefined: (1) ordering across the cell→central-index boundary — Kafka gives per-partition order only, so 'per-tenant partitioned' lets a grant-revoke and a re-publish for the same record reorder if they traverse different keys; (2) idempotency/exactly-once on replay — Debezium snapshots and connector restarts replay events; a non-idempotent index applier can RESURRECT a revoked or downgraded record; (3) the interaction between the synchronous revocation authority and CDC-eventual index membership is unspecified — revocation of a SHARED record (un-sharing) is a shared-tier CDC event and therefore eventual, NOT synchronous, so there is an unbounded window where an un-shared record stays discoverable across the federation.
why: Revocation of a SHARED record is the common case (Feature #1 discovery + revocable grants, locked). If shared-tier revocation is CDC-eventual with no bounded staleness and no idempotent-apply guarantee, you have a stale-discovery leak: an institution un-shares a profile/paper and it stays discoverable for an unbounded window, or reappears after a connector replay. This violates 'explicit, revocable sharing grants' as a first-class enforced concept.
fix: Specify the index consistency contract: (a) every index record carries the PublishableProjection version + grant epoch; the applier is idempotent and MONOTONIC (rejects lower-epoch applies, so replays cannot resurrect); (b) post-filter shared-tier discovery results against the fast revocation bitmap/epoch at query time — promote the bitmap from 'perf only' to a correctness gate for shared-tier discovery so CDC lag never yields a stale HIT; (c) state and test a bounded staleness SLO for share→discoverable and revoke→undiscoverable. Make CDC ordering per-(tenant, record) key, not per-tenant, so grant/revoke/republish for one record are totally ordered.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation + exchange + control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The TierLattice 'exhaustiveness via signed conformance attestation per consumer + control plane refuses activation until all attest + runtime fail-closed deny' (Risk #30) is a distributed-coordination mechanism masquerading as a maintainability fix, with two new hazards. LIVENESS: 'control plane refuses activation until ALL consumers attest' makes a single stale/offline/slow consumer node block a tier-lattice change platform-wide — a global barrier across N autonomous university nodes you don't operationally control; in a federation you will never get all nodes to attest promptly, so it stalls forever or you add a timeout that silently degrades to the non-exhaustiveness it was meant to prevent. CORRECTNESS: no protocol prevents a window where some nodes know the new lattice and others don't, during which cross-node tier comparisons (the MAX-rule, join semantics) operate on DIFFERENT lattices — a correctness fault in the confidentiality computation itself.
why: The TierLattice is the algebraic core of confidentiality (MAX-rule, sticky flags, joins). A heterogeneous-lattice window across federated nodes means two nodes can disagree on whether a derived/joined artifact is confidential — a leak or over-block, both correctness failures of a locked requirement. And 'refuse activation until all attest' is an availability footgun in a system whose premise is autonomous, intermittently-connected nodes.
fix: Make tier-lattice changes ADDITIVE and versioned with a min-node-version floor (you already use this in Risk #36): an un-upgraded node treats unknown tiers as MOST-restrictive (fail-closed), so heterogeneous lattices are safe-by-construction, not blocked-by-barrier. Drop 'refuse activation until all attest'; replace with 'gate FEATURES that emit a new tier on the consuming node's attested version,' so an un-upgraded node simply can't receive new-tier records, never blocks the platform, never compares on a stale lattice. Specify a two-phase rollout: all nodes RECOGNIZE the value (fail-closed default) before any node EMITS it.

lens: Distributed-systems & scalability: federation + exchange + control/data-plane split, consistency model, sync mechanics (CDC/events), bottlenecks, failure domains, hot tenants, KG + vector-index growth, cross-node latency.
issue: The federated knowledge-graph and central-index GROWTH/cost is not actually costed — it is deferred to Open Questions #7 and #9 ('cost the materialized central graph at N=10/50/200', 'cost the re-clustering migration') with no numbers, no growth model, no scaling analysis in the plan body. 'Materialized public-tier central graph' + 'managed central index (hybrid topical-macro + per-tenant sub-partition)' is a centralized component growing with (institutions × researchers × papers × embeddings). At N=200 R1 universities the public-tier corpus is plausibly tens of millions of papers/profiles with multi-vector embeddings (SPECTER2 + BGE/Qwen3) — a vector index in the 10s-100s GB to TB range plus a property graph of similar cardinality. The plan asserts 'bounded bitmap stays in a bounded shard set' and 'per-shard admission control' but gives no memory/cost ceiling, no re-embedding cost model when the embedding model changes (a 'first-class migration' that re-embeds the ENTIRE central corpus — a multi-day GPU job scaling with corpus size), and no answer to whether the materialized central graph is one logical store (HA/scaling bottleneck) or itself sharded.
why: The central index/graph is the one component NOT cell-isolated and therefore the one place where 'horizontally scalable' (NFR) is actually tested under cross-tenant aggregate load. If its growth model is unknown, the COGS floor (Risk #37, '$3-5k/mo') is unfalsifiable at scale — plausibly a single-node-era figure; idle-index OCU floor for a TB-scale managed vector index at N=200 is far higher. Whole-corpus re-embedding is presented as 'exercised once on synthetic data' but its cost grows linearly and recurs every model upgrade.
fix: Put a real growth model in the plan body, not the appendix: corpus-size estimates per node tier, embedding dim × count → index bytes, graph node/edge cardinality → store size, recurring re-embedding cost as a function of corpus size × upgrade cadence. Decide explicitly whether the central index/graph is itself sharded+replicated (across how many nodes) or a single managed instance — and if single, name the vertical ceiling and the N at which it breaks. Tie COGS to the growth model so '$3-5k/mo' is a function of N, not a constant.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The entire confidentiality lattice is rooted in a classifier, yet Phase-0 ships a SINGLE fail-closed classifier with no specified default-deny semantics for the unclassifiable/ambiguous case, and no human-in-the-loop adjudication. 'Fail-closed' is asserted but undefined: fail-closed to WHICH tier? If an unclassified record defaults to 'confidential' the product is unusable (everything locks); if it defaults to anything less, a misclassified confidential record is silently public/private-indexed and leaks. The plan never states the default tier for classifier abstention, and dual-classifier + agreement-for-tier-down is explicitly deferred to Phase 1 (Risk #25, Phase-0 build list). So the beachhead's confidential lab data is protected by one model's point estimate with no second opinion and no quarantine-on-uncertainty.
why: Misclassification is the root leak path for a classification-driven system: every downstream control (router locality, egress PEP, index admission, MAX-rule) trusts the tier label. A false 'public' on a confidential record bypasses ALL of them simultaneously and the leak is silent — there is no detector because every control downstream believes the label. For an industry-NDA lab (the named beachhead), one such leak is a contract-terminating, possibly litigable event. Shipping single-classifier to the exact buyer whose value proposition IS confidentiality is the highest-stakes corner cut in the plan.
fix: Define classifier-abstention semantics explicitly: any record the single classifier cannot confidently label MUST quarantine to a default-deny 'unclassified=confidential, excluded-from-all-retrieval' state requiring human adjudication before it enters any index — Phase 0, not Phase 1. Make the confidence threshold and the human-review queue part of the Phase-0 build, not the dual-classifier deferral. Add a Phase-0 contract test: inject low-confidence and adversarial records (e.g., confidential content with public-looking metadata) and assert zero leak into any retrievable surface. The dual classifier can wait; the default-deny-on-abstention semantics cannot.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The central index privacy bound is gated on a red-team (Risk #1, #5, #8) but the index is BUILT and POPULATED before the bound is proven, and embeddings are irreversibly published once written. 'GA-GATED on the LSH/membership red-team' gates the GA launch, not the data creation. If the red-team in Phase 2 finds that presence/count/churn DP-noise + k-anonymity + per-consortium scope-keys do NOT hold the claimed bound (a likely outcome — embedding-inversion and membership-inference against cross-tenant indices is an open research problem, not a solved one), the public/shared embeddings are already in a shared central store and cannot be un-published. There is no specified rollback of already-centralized embeddings, and the MAX-rule only prevents confidential-DERIVED content — it does not prevent membership/linkage inference across PUBLIC-tier embeddings that, in aggregate, re-identify confidential collaborations.
why: This is a one-way door deferred past its point of no return. The plan's own load-bearing assumption ('no confidential-derived content in the index') does not defend against the subtler attack: correlating public-tier presence/churn signals across consortium members to infer the EXISTENCE and membership of a confidential project (e.g., two institutions' public embeddings suddenly co-cluster → infer an undisclosed joint confidential effort). That is a confidentiality leak via metadata even with a perfect MAX-rule. A DP bound that is wrong after embeddings ship is unrecoverable.
fix: Prove the privacy bound on SYNTHETIC/de-identified corpora BEFORE any real embedding is written to the shared central index — move the membership-inference + embedding-inversion + cross-tenant-linkage red-team to a Phase-1 gate on the index design, not a Phase-2 GA gate on the launch. Specify the quantified resistance bound (ε, k, churn-noise) in shared/contracts/ as the plan promises, AND specify the rollback/re-embed procedure if the bound is later found insufficient. Explicitly add cross-tenant co-occurrence/linkage inference (not just per-record inversion) to the threat model — the current red-team scope (#5, #8) is per-record and misses the aggregate-correlation attack.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: BYO-confidential-endpoint verification is claimed via 'in-boundary network proof + mTLS model-identity + no-retention enforcement' (Risk #12), but no-retention is not technically enforceable against a remote provider. mTLS proves you are talking to a claimed endpoint; a network-egress proof shows traffic stays in a boundary you control — but if the BYO endpoint is the institution's OWN cloud account (the whole point of BYO), the institution's provider can log/retain prompts and completions and you cannot cryptographically prevent or detect it. 'No-retention enforcement' against a third party is a contractual control dressed as a technical one — exactly the overclaim the plan correctly calls out for at-rest (HYOK) but reintroduces here for inference.
why: Confidential data sent to a BYO inference endpoint is plaintext-at-use at a boundary the platform does not control. For export-controlled (ITAR/EAR) or EU-personal data, an unverifiable no-retention claim is a compliance fiction — a foreign-person-accessible log of ITAR data is a deemed-export violation regardless of contract. The plan forbids BYO for export/EU-personal 'unless sovereign-verified' but does not define what sovereign-verified means technically, leaving the strongest-confidentiality buyers depending on an undefined control.
fix: Relabel no-retention as a CONTRACTUAL control with a TEE-attestation technical upgrade path, matching the honesty applied to HYOK-at-rest. For export/EU-personal, require TEE-with-remote-attestation on the BYO endpoint (the provider proves enclave + no-log via attestation), and if the BYO provider cannot attest, the router MUST refuse and fall back to the in-boundary self-hosted model — make that a fail-closed routing rule in the policy table (Risk #33's single routing table), not a contractual caveat. Define 'sovereign-verified' concretely (attested enclave + jurisdiction proof + named-person access controls) in shared/contracts/.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: The confused-deputy / IDOR defense for cross-institution brokered drill-down is named ('per-tenant-signed, HSM-DPoP, confused-deputy-safe') but not specified, and the trust model between two mutually-distrusting institutions at the exchange is underspecified. When Institution A requests drill-down into Institution B's shared confidential artifact, WHO performs the authz check and on WHOSE assertions? The plan says 'owner-side authoritative determination' and 'grantee attributes untrusted hints' (Risk #9) for export, which is correct — but it does not generalize that to the brokered-path authz: the broker must not be confusable into using A's identity to access B's data beyond the grant scope, and B must re-derive the grant scope from ITS OWN policy, never from the broker's or A's claim. With per-tenant-signed tokens, a malicious or compromised exchange node could mint or replay a token for a scope it was not granted unless B independently validates scope against its own grant store on every request.
why: This is the classic federation confused-deputy: the exchange/broker is a deputy with credentials to talk to many tenants; if any tenant's authz trusts the broker's framing of the request rather than re-deriving authority from its own grant store, a single compromised or buggy broker becomes a cross-institution IDOR that defeats the entire isolation guarantee. DPoP binds a token to a key but does not by itself prevent scope confusion if the resource server trusts the presented scope. The plan's strongest authz statement (owner-side determination, untrusted grantee hints) is scoped to export only, not made the universal rule.
fix: Make 'owner-side authoritative re-derivation of grant scope, broker/grantee assertions are untrusted hints' the UNIVERSAL invariant for ALL cross-tenant access, not just export US-person checks. Specify: every brokered drill-down request carries a grant-ID; the OWNER node looks up that grant in its own authoritative grant store, re-derives scope + tier + caveats + revocation status, and ignores any scope claim in the token. Add a Phase-1 contract test (the plan has 'grantee-over-assert-denied' — good, but add 'broker-over-assert-denied' and 'replayed-token-for-revoked-grant-denied'). Specify token audience-binding and single-use/short-TTL + the synchronous revocation check (Risk #2) on the SAME path so a replayed token hits a live deny.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: Audit integrity defends against tampering by external parties and divergence between nodes (hash-chain, fair-exchange receipts, external timestamp, owner-WORM, divergence auto-suspend — Risk #13) but does NOT specify a defense against a malicious or compelled NODE OPERATOR rewriting its OWN local audit chain before any cross-node receipt exists. In a federated model each university operates its own node and owns its own audit log; the hash-chain is only tamper-EVIDENT to a verifier who holds a prior anchor. For purely-internal confidential access (no cross-institution receipt generated), there is no external anchor, so a node operator (or an attacker with node-admin) can truncate/rewrite the local chain and re-seal it. The plan's 'shared external timestamp' and 'fair-exchange receipts' only cover DISCLOSURE events, not internal confidential access.
why: FERPA/GDPR/export audit obligations require a non-repudiable record of WHO accessed WHAT — including internal access. If the operator who is also the data controller can silently rewrite internal-access history, the audit log fails its core compliance purpose precisely where it matters most for an insider-threat or a compelled-access scenario. The repudiation defense is asymmetric: strong across institutions, weak within one.
fix: Anchor every node's audit chain to an external, operator-independent commitment on a regular cadence (e.g., periodic signed chain-head checkpoints to the exchange/control plane and/or a public transparency log / RFC 3161 TSA), so the chain is tamper-evident even for internal-only access and even against the node operator. Specify the checkpoint interval as the maximum undetectable-rewrite window and make it a compliance-tier parameter. Add insider/operator threat to the audit threat model explicitly — the current model assumes the node operator is trusted, which contradicts the multi-institution mutual-distrust premise.

lens: Security, privacy & confidentiality: end-to-end enforcement of the public/private/confidential model, authz completeness (ABAC+ReBAC), cross-institution sharing/revocation, confidential-tier crypto/BYOK, federation confused-deputy/IDOR risks, and FERPA/GDPR/export-control correctness.
issue: Revocation correctness is well-handled on the SYNCHRONOUS confidential hot path, but the plan explicitly accepts a 'bounded ALLOW window' for lower tiers (private/shared non-high-sensitivity) and for the durable-buffer recovery path under authority outage (Risk #2, #4, Q4). A bounded allow-window means that after a grant is revoked, private/shared data remains accessible to the (former) grantee for up to the window (stated as 5s or 30s). For a revocation triggered by a SECURITY event (compromised grantee account, terminated employee, withdrawn consent under GDPR Art. 7(3), FERPA consent withdrawal), a 30s — or worse, a recovery-measured — allow window is a window of unauthorized access to private/personal data. The plan honestly labels it, which I credit, but honesty about a leak is not closure of the leak.
why: GDPR consent withdrawal and FERPA consent revocation are supposed to take effect promptly; a documented 30s (or longer-under-degradation) window where revoked personal data is still served is a documented compliance gap, and 'we labeled it' is not a defense to a regulator or a DPA. More dangerously, the allow-window for the recovery path is 'recovery-measured' — i.e., potentially unbounded during a prolonged authority outage for lower tiers, which means a multi-hour control-plane outage = multi-hour stale-grant serving of private data.
fix: Distinguish revocation REASON, not just tier: any revocation flagged security/consent-withdrawal/compromise MUST take the synchronous deny-correct path regardless of tier (treat it like high-sensitivity), so the allow-window applies only to benign administrative revocations of low-sensitivity public/shared data. Cap the recovery-path allow-window with a hard ceiling that triggers fail-closed for personal-data tiers when exceeded, rather than 'recovery-measured.' State the GDPR/FERPA-facing maximum-staleness commitment explicitly per tier and reason, and confirm it with the DPO (Q13) and registrar (Q14) — the current validation questions ask about gating correctness but not about the acceptability of the allow-window staleness for withdrawn consent.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: The cross-cutting confidentiality enforcement makes the 'pluggable module, minimal blast radius' requirement structurally unachievable. Confidentiality is enforced via at least seven mechanisms that every feature must cooperate with: fail-closed classifier, MAX-rule PublishableProjection, sticky-UNION compliance flags on joins/derivatives, synchronous revocation check on the hot path, source-class re-derivation, owner-side determinations, and per-consumer conformance attestation. Any new feature module (e.g. mod-funding consuming IExpertiseFingerprint/ICollaborationGraph in Phase 3) must correctly thread ALL of these or it becomes a leak vector. There is no single enforcement boundary a module can sit behind; enforcement is smeared across the data plane. Adding feature #4 has maximal, not minimal, blast radius — it must re-derive source class, propagate sticky flags, respect projection rules, and ship a conformance attestation.
why: Directly contradicts the locked NON-FUNCTIONAL requirement ('features are pluggable modules added/removed with minimal blast radius') AND the locked confidentiality requirement simultaneously. The plan treats these as independently satisfiable; they are coupled. A new module cannot be reasoned about in isolation, which is the definition of a non-modular system. This is the rot that sinks the 'modular platform' thesis.
fix: Make confidentiality enforcement a mediated chokepoint, not a contract every module reimplements. Force ALL retrieval/egress/derivation through a single Policy Enforcement Point service (the egress PEP) and a single data-access broker such that feature modules physically CANNOT touch classified data except through the broker — i.e. modules receive already-projected, already-tier-checked result objects and never see raw classification logic. Prove it with a 'module cannot import the raw store / cannot construct a PublishableProjection' import-linter + runtime contract. Then adding mod-funding inherits enforcement for free instead of re-threading seven invariants.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: The shared contracts/kernel package is the central coupling point and the plan has no contract-evolution mechanism that scales to the four-feature, multi-tenant, multi-version reality. Risk 30 (TierLattice exhaustiveness) is 'resolved' by requiring a signed conformance attestation per consumer + control-plane refuses activation until all attest. This means every TierLattice change (adding a tier, changing a code-classification, refining the MAX-rule) requires re-attestation from EVERY consumer across EVERY node before activation. With N federated nodes each running possibly different versions, a single lattice change is a global, synchronized, all-consumers-must-cooperate migration. That is the highest-coupling possible design for the most security-critical shared type.
why: The TierLattice and shared contracts are load-bearing for confidentiality (locked) AND for modularity (locked). If every change to them is a fleet-wide blocking migration, the platform ossifies: nobody dares change the lattice, so confidentiality posture freezes at v1, and feature modules accrete workarounds. This violates maintainability at the exact point the architecture is most safety-critical. 'Control plane refuses activation until all attest' is a global liveness coupling masquerading as a safety property.
fix: Separate the lattice's SAFETY semantics (must be globally consistent) from its EXTENSIBILITY (per-feature classification codes). Make the core lattice a tiny, near-frozen, formally specified type (3 tiers + ordering) and push feature-specific classification into per-module policy that maps onto the frozen lattice. Define an explicit lattice-version negotiation: nodes advertise supported lattice versions; the exchange operates at the min-common version; new tiers are additive-with-default-deny so a non-upgraded consumer fails closed WITHOUT blocking the upgrade of others. Get rid of the synchronous all-attest-before-activation gate for additive changes.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: 'Extraction = own DB + event-bus-only' is stated as the modular-monolith-to-services escape hatch (Risk 32), but there is NO event contract design anywhere: no event schema/versioning, no ordering/idempotency guarantees, no consumer-group/replay semantics, no schema registry, and Kafka is explicitly deferred out of Phase 0 and only appears in Phase 2 as CDC plumbing. So the declared decoupling mechanism does not exist when the first extraction would happen, and CDC (Debezium reading Postgres) is data-coupling, not an event contract. A module extracted this way reads another module's table changes — that is the distributed monolith the plan claims to avoid.
why: The entire 'modular monolith now, services later, blast radius stays small' story rests on a clean extraction seam. If the seam is 'Debezium off a shared Postgres', extracted services are coupled to internal table schemas of the module they left behind. Changing that module's schema breaks the extracted service silently. The plan's own Risk 32 mitigation reintroduces Risk 32.
fix: Define module-to-module communication as published domain EVENTS with an explicit versioned schema (e.g. a registry + Avro/Protobuf + compatibility rules) from Phase 0, even inside the monolith (in-process event bus with the same contract). CDC/Debezium is acceptable ONLY for replicating a module's OWN data to its OWN read store, never as a cross-module integration API. State idempotency, ordering, and replay guarantees per event topic. Without this, do not claim 'event-bus-only' as a decoupling property.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: import-linter is the ONLY isolation mechanism for the entire Phase-0 monolith ('consolidated Postgres with import-linter isolation only', explicitly NO DB-role isolation until Phase 1). import-linter constrains Python import direction only. It cannot stop module A from reading module B's tables via the shared connection, cannot enforce data ownership, cannot prevent shared mutable state, and cannot enforce transaction boundaries. So the Phase-0 'module boundaries' are enforceable only at the import graph, while the real coupling (shared schema, shared transactions, shared session) is unconstrained.
why: This is exactly how modular monoliths rot into big balls of mud: the import graph stays clean while the database becomes a shared global variable. By the time Phase-1 tries to add per-module schemas + DB-role grants, every module will already depend on cross-reading tables, making the extraction the plan promised is cheap into an expensive retrofit. The 'blast radius' claim is false at the data layer for the entire MVP.
fix: Even in the consolidated Postgres, enforce per-module schemas + per-module DB roles with REVOKE on other modules' schemas from Phase 0 (this is nearly free — it's DDL + GRANT, not new infra). That makes data ownership a checkable boundary on day one and makes Phase-1 extraction mechanical. Pair import-linter with a 'no cross-schema query' lint/runtime check. Do not defer DB-role isolation to Phase 1 — that defers the only enforcement that actually preserves the extraction seam.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: The AI model-router's swappability is asserted but undercut by the tier→locality coupling and BYO-provider requirement, with no model-capability abstraction. Risk 33 mandates a 'single tier→locality routing table both router and transport consult.' Routing is therefore by DATA CLASS, not by model CAPABILITY. But the locked AI requirement also demands provider-agnostic routing AND bring-your-own-provider-per-institution. There is no stated abstraction for 'what can this model do' (context window, embedding dim, tool-use, structured-output, cost) — so adding a new provider or a tenant's BYO endpoint means the router must learn provider-specific quirks, and the locality table must be re-reconciled per tenant. Embedding-model swaps (nomic->Qwen3/BGE in Risk 40) change vector dimensionality, which ripples into the index, the projection codes, and the membership-inference bound (Risk 5/8) — a cross-cutting change with no migration contract.
why: Provider-agnostic + BYO-per-institution is a locked requirement and a core sell. If the router couples model selection to a global locality table without a capability/cost abstraction, every new provider or tenant key is a router edit with unbounded blast radius, and embedding swaps silently invalidate the central index's privacy guarantees. 'Swappable' is claimed but not structurally true.
fix: Define an explicit ModelProvider interface (capabilities, locality-class it satisfies, cost, retention/no-train attestation, embedding dim if applicable) and make the router select over a registry of providers that DECLARE which locality classes they satisfy — rather than hardcoding tier->locality. BYO endpoints register as providers with attested locality. Treat embedding-model identity as a versioned property of the index with an explicit re-embed migration contract, and pin the membership-inference bound to (embedding-model-version, index-version) so a swap is gated, not silent.

lens: Maintainability & modularity: real module boundaries, decoupling, blast radius, interface clarity, testability, monorepo vs services, swappable AI-router and retrieval
issue: Testability of the federation/revocation/PEP core depends on a 'stub = thin adapter over REAL contract artifacts' plus a 'per-release contract test vs ephemeral real control plane' (Risk 34). This is the single most security-critical and distributed part of the system, and the test strategy is one ephemeral-real-control-plane integration test per release. There is no stated component-level contract test suite, no consumer-driven contract testing between the cell and exchange, no property-based testing of the TierLattice/MAX-rule, and the synchronous-revocation 'deny-correct (commit-1s-ago)' test is a single named milestone, not a continuous invariant suite. The hardest-to-test behaviors (clock skew, partition, authority outage, bulk-revoke saturation) are listed as one-time milestone verifications, not regression-protected contracts.
why: Confidentiality is locked and the federation seam is the existential risk (Risk 1/2). If its correctness is protected by once-per-release integration tests against an ephemeral environment rather than fast, deterministic contract + property tests, regressions in tier propagation or revocation ordering will not be caught until late, and refactoring the kernel (which the plan WANTS to do via eviction, Risk 31) becomes terrifying. Hard-to-test = won't-be-refactored = rot, precisely in the safety-critical core.
fix: Specify consumer-driven contract tests (e.g. Pact-style) between every cell and the exchange so each side's expectations are versioned and CI-enforced. Make the TierLattice MAX-rule, sticky-flag UNION, and source-class re-derivation property-tested (generate random tier/flag combinations, assert the published projection never exceeds source class). Encode partition/skew/revoke-ordering as deterministic simulation tests (a la deterministic-sim or injected-clock harness) that run every CI, not as one milestone. Without continuous invariant tests, the eviction/refactor plan (Risk 31) is unsafe to execute.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: The Phase-0 revenue wedge is a single-tenant confidential-RAG appliance with NONE of the locked federation/cross-institution value, and the plan explicitly concedes it 'does NOT inherit the federation moat.' First revenue therefore validates demand for a commodity 'secure RAG over my lab's docs' product — a category already served by Glean, NotebookLM Enterprise, Azure OpenAI on-your-data, Microsoft Copilot, and a dozen vertical secure-RAG vendors — not for the federated platform the company is actually betting on.
why: If the only revenue-bearing thing you can ship is the part with no moat, you are funding the company on a feature that incumbents can match in a quarter, while the differentiated platform (federation) remains entirely unvalidated by paying customers. The KILL-GATE proves a lab will pay $30k for secure RAG — it proves NOTHING about whether institutions will pay for cross-institution confidential collaboration, which is the whole thesis. You can pass Phase-0 with flying colors and still have a dead platform.
fix: Add a Phase-0 *demand* gate for the federation thesis itself, not just the appliance: in the same Week-1 interviews, secure written evidence that the SAME buyers have a concrete, funded cross-institution confidential-sharing pain (a named collaboration they cannot currently do, with a budget owner). If you cannot find a buyer who wants BOTH the box AND the federation, the federation thesis is unvalidated regardless of Phase-0 revenue — say so and treat Phase-1 as a second, independent kill-gate with its own pivot option, not a build-order continuation.

lens: Product, GTM & business viability
issue: Cross-institution network-effect bootstrapping (cold-start) is hand-waved. The entire tier-3 (shared confidential workspaces) and tier-4 (cross-institution grant team assembly) value, plus the central public-tier graph, are worthless at N=1 and marginal at N=2. The plan's only cold-start instruments are non-binding 'framework LOIs' and a presumed 'structural disincentive' that keeps incumbents out. An LOI is not a deployed node, does not generate cross-institution data, and does not de-risk the dyad's master-agreement (which the plan itself flags as a separate falsification risk).
why: Federated platforms die in the cold-start gap: institution A won't deploy because there's no one to federate with; institution B says the same. The cross-institution features need a PRE-EXISTING reason for two specific institutions to share confidential data (an active multi-site grant, an industry consortium, a clinical network) — and the named beachhead (industry-NDA labs) is precisely the segment LEAST likely to want to federate confidential IP with a peer institution. The plan sells the box to buyers structurally hostile to the federation feature, then hopes to retrofit federation. That is the textbook bootstrapping failure.
